EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed in eastern Colorado during the afternoon moving northeast behind an outflow boundary. As the storms entered into southwest Nebraska, they merged with thunderstorms that developed in northeast Colorado to form a line of storms along the outflow boundary. This line of storms produced hail up to penny sized sized in Dundy County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to penny sized hail reported at the location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief landspout tornado formed in southern Dundy County. The tornado appeared to form as a storm was developing as it moved east northeast towards Benkelman, ahead of an incoming line of storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dundy County Dispatch received a report of a possible landspout 10 miles west of Benkelman. Landspout was in contact with the ground for 30 seconds. A review of radar data determined this event did occur as weather conditions were favorable for development.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level trough in split flow moved out of the southwestern U.S. on the morning of April 12, and phased with a strong northern stream trough across the northern Plains by April 13. Intense low pressure developed across the central Plains and moved into the Great Lakes, with a strong frontogenetic band of precipitation from Nebraska to Minnesota. Convective instability produced some spotty thunder along with the rain changing to snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.0 inches at Newcastle and 3.5 inches at Concord. Winds near Wayne and Gayville gusted from 35 to 42 mph at times between 0700CST and 1400CST, with visibilities frequently at one-half mile or less, and occasionally as low as one-quarter mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level trough in split flow moved out of the southwestern U.S. on the morning of April 12, and phased with a strong northern stream trough across the northern Plains by April 13. Intense low pressure developed across the central Plains and moved into the Great Lakes, with a strong frontogenetic band of precipitation from Nebraska to Minnesota. Convective instability produced some spotty thunder along with the rain changing to snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 2.3 inches at Emerson. Winds at Sioux City gusted frequently to 35 to 40 mph between 0830-1530, and led to frequent visibility under one mile, and as low as one-half mile at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid April severe/winter storm system came through Nebraska over the weekend on April 11 and 12. Before the blast of cold air, a surge of warm, moist air in front of a rapidly strengthening low led to severe weather across southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa. 3 inch hail was reported near Murray, IA. The next day a cold front moved through the area and the primary precipitation type was snow. A strong pressure gradient led to strong winds with a 58 MPH the max wind gust reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer reported 0.2 inches of snow. Snow reports were lacking. It was Easter, the snow was lighter than expected, and difficult to measure with winds 40-50 mph throughout the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe weather produced numerous wind and hail reports across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 25 to 50 percent of normal during July and August with above normal temperatures led to the development of severe short-term drought conditions over much of northeast Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate (D1) drought evolved to severe (D2) drought conditions by mid-month. Rainfall of less than 50 percent of normal along with above normal temperatures contributed to the worsening short-term agricultural drought. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An initially unimpressive thunderstorm complex over northeast Nebraska rapidly intensified during the morning of August 10, 2020. After a few sporadic early morning damage reports, the intensifying storms began to more readily produce damaging winds in excess of 50 kts around 1340Z. Those damaging winds increased in coverage and intensity as the storms moved east into Iowa. The portion of the thunderstorm complex that moved through the NWS Omaha forecast area marked the approximate beginning of an intense derecho that tracked across portions of Iowa, Illinois, Wisconsin, Indiana, Michigan, and Ohio. Within the NWS Omaha forecast area, there were more than 20 local storm reports of wind damage and measured severe wind gusts, including more than 50,000 power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public personal weather station measured a wind of 54 knots.

Those damaging winds increased in coverage and intensity as the storms moved east into Iowa. The portion of the thunderstorm complex that moved through the NWS Omaha forecast area marked the approximate beginning of an intense derecho that tracked across portions of Iowa, Illinois, Wisconsin, Indiana, Michigan, and Ohio. Within the NWS Omaha forecast area, there were more than 20 local storm reports of wind damage and measured severe wind gusts, including more than 50,000 power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Former NWS Employee called in estimating winds at 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local mesonet measured 63 MPH wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved north and produced a brief tornado and wind damage. A cold front quickly followed the warm front and let to several reports of 50+ mph non-thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported uprooted trees. Limbs up to 4 inches were broken off. Buildings were damaged from downed tree branches. There were numerous power outages in the Fairbury area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level short wave came off of the Rocky Mountains out over the central Plains. This led to a surface low developing over Kansas. In front of that surface low, warm moist air was advected north. Isentropic upglide and led to an overrunning precipitation event that led to some high snowfall totals in eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa. It was the #5 (5.4 inches) and #4 (4.5 inches) highest April snowfall events for Omaha and Lincoln respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from the public for 6 inches of snowfall from Blair, NE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions remained unchanged across western and north central Nebraska in December. Precipitation was below normal in the eastern Nebraska Panhandle and portions of far southwestern Nebraska. Across the northern Sandhills from eastern Cherry county into Holt county, precipitation for the month was running 50 to 90 percent of normal. Across the central and southern Sandhills, including Lincoln and Keith counties, precipitation was 100 to 150 percent of normal. For the year ending in December, Broken Bow, North Platte, Valentine and Imperial, ran precipitation deficits of: -3.33, -5.48, -1.94, and -9.36 inches respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the month of December, drought severity remained unchanged across Garden County. Extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted over the western 2/3rds of Garden county with severe (D2) drought conditions over the eastern third of the county. The cooperative station at Lisco reported 0.21 inches of precipitation for the month. This was 0.42 inches below the normal of 0.63 inches.  The cooperative station at Oshkosh reported 0.19 inches of precipitation for the month. This was 0.18 inches below the normal of 0.37 inches. The cooperative station at Crescent Lakes National Wildlife Refuge reported 0.33 inches of precipitation for the month. This was 0.04 inches below the normal of 0.37 inches. The cooperative station at Oshkosh 8SW reported 0.34 inches of precipitation for the month. This was 0.16 inches below the normal of 0.50 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level short wave came off of the Rocky Mountains out over the central Plains. This led to a surface low developing over Kansas. In front of that surface low, warm moist air was advected north. Isentropic upglide and led to an overrunning precipitation event that led to some high snowfall totals in eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa. It was the #5 (5.4 inches) and #4 (4.5 inches) highest April snowfall events for Omaha and Lincoln respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum snow report from public was 8 inches of snow.

Drought conditions remained unchanged across western and north central Nebraska in December. Precipitation was below normal in the eastern Nebraska Panhandle and portions of far southwestern Nebraska. Across the northern Sandhills from eastern Cherry county into Holt county, precipitation for the month was running 50 to 90 percent of normal. Across the central and southern Sandhills, including Lincoln and Keith counties, precipitation was 100 to 150 percent of normal. For the year ending in December, Broken Bow, North Platte, Valentine and Imperial, ran precipitation deficits of: -3.33, -5.48, -1.94, and -9.36 inches respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the month of December, drought severity remained unchanged across Sheridan County. The western half of the county was in extreme (D3) drought with the eastern half of the county in severe (D2) drought. The cooperative station at Gordon 6N reported 0.62 inches of precipitation for the month. This was 0.17 inches above the normal of 0.45 inches. The cooperative station at Ellsworth 2W reported 0.34 inches of precipitation for the month. This was 0.11 inches above the normal of 0.23 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level disturbance worked northeast from Colorado early morning January 12 into Iowa by late day.  Broad lift initially within the warm advection zone became increasingly concentrated, with deeper lift in the dendritic growth layer by afternoon across northwest Iowa.  Snowfall rates up to 1.5 to 2 inches per hour were noted across northwest Iowa and northeast Nebraska at the peak during the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to briefly heavy snowfall during the afternoon accumulated to 2.5 inches at Emerson and South Sioux City, resulting in hazardous road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early morning heavy banded snowfall moved across the central and northeast portions of the Nebraska Panhandle producing high snowfall rates of up to 6 inches per hour based on overnight storm reports. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer located twelve miles south of Redington measured a storm total snowfall of 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread wet snowfall of 3 to 5 inches blanketed nearly all of south central Nebraska on this Friday. This snowfall was associated with a weak and non-strengthening short-wave trough. So, the radar presentation of this event was disorganized. Small patches of light snow began developing from Colorado into Kansas and southern Nebraska during the morning hours. Due to dry low-level air, it took several hours for precipitation to reach the ground on a consistent basis. By midday, a relatively well-organized area of light snow had formed over northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska. This area moved east-northeast across south central Nebraska during the mid-to-late afternoon. Despite the cloud cover, temperatures had climbed into the low 40s east of Hwy 183, before precipitation began falling. That allowed for some light rain to fall in some areas before the column rapidly cooled, resulting in a changeover to snow. During the daylight hours, the only areas where measurable snow fell were southwest and west of Grand Island, with between 1 and 2 inches of accumulation on grassy areas. Elsewhere, actual air temperature and ground temperature allowed the snow to melt on contact. That area of snow moved into eastern Nebraska by sunset, but in its wake, more patchy areas of snow continued to develop  through the evening. After sunset, snow began accumulating across the rest of the area. By midnight, a fairly large but unorganized area of precipitation enveloped much of the Central Plains, extending from southern South Dakota to Oklahoma. Across south central Nebraska, the snow did fill-in after midnight, becoming steadier, but with diminishing intensity. The snow ended across the area by 8 AM Saturday. There was one isolated report of 6 inches in Minden. Otherwise the highest reports included 5.4 inches in Bradshaw, and 5.0 inches at Palmer, Elwood, and Bladen. Impacts from this snowfall were minimal as it occurred on a Friday night. Schools were not in session and no morning or evening commutes were involved. Due to the recent warm weather, snow struggled to accumulate on roads and parking lots, which also helped. The wet, heavy character of the snow coated all the trees like cotton candy and resulted in a 6-inch diameter tree branch being snapped off near Shelby. ||There was no low pressure system associated with this snowfall. Strong high pressure was over central Canada and extended southeast into the Ohio Valley. A cold front was stationary from the Gulf Coast states across Texas, with its western extent stationary, banked up against the Rockies. In the upper-levels, the flow across the U.S. was west-southwest, with a subtropical high over the Gulf of Mexico. This weak shortwave trough came out of the Desert Southwest, and it weakened further as it crossed the Central Plains. By sunset Saturday, it was a minimal dip in the 500 mb heights over the Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches across the county.

This area moved east-northeast across south central Nebraska during the mid-to-late afternoon. Despite the cloud cover, temperatures had climbed into the low 40s east of Hwy 183, before precipitation began falling. That allowed for some light rain to fall in some areas before the column rapidly cooled, resulting in a changeover to snow. During the daylight hours, the only areas where measurable snow fell were southwest and west of Grand Island, with between 1 and 2 inches of accumulation on grassy areas. Elsewhere, actual air temperature and ground temperature allowed the snow to melt on contact. That area of snow moved into eastern Nebraska by sunset, but in its wake, more patchy areas of snow continued to develop  through the evening. After sunset, snow began accumulating across the rest of the area. By midnight, a fairly large but unorganized area of precipitation enveloped much of the Central Plains, extending from southern South Dakota to Oklahoma. Across south central Nebraska, the snow did fill-in after midnight, becoming steadier, but with diminishing intensity. The snow ended across the area by 8 AM Saturday. There was one isolated report of 6 inches in Minden. Otherwise the highest reports included 5.4 inches in Bradshaw, and 5.0 inches at Palmer, Elwood, and Bladen. Impacts from this snowfall were minimal as it occurred on a Friday night. Schools were not in session and no morning or evening commutes were involved. Due to the recent warm weather, snow struggled to accumulate on roads and parking lots, which also helped. The wet, heavy character of the snow coated all the trees like cotton candy and resulted in a 6-inch diameter tree branch being snapped off near Shelby. ||There was no low pressure system associated with this snowfall. Strong high pressure was over central Canada and extended southeast into the Ohio Valley. A cold front was stationary from the Gulf Coast states across Texas, with its western extent stationary, banked up against the Rockies. In the upper-levels, the flow across the U.S. was west-southwest, with a subtropical high over the Gulf of Mexico. This weak shortwave trough came out of the Desert Southwest, and it weakened further as it crossed the Central Plains. By sunset Saturday, it was a minimal dip in the 500 mb heights over the Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches across the county.

That allowed for some light rain to fall in some areas before the column rapidly cooled, resulting in a changeover to snow. During the daylight hours, the only areas where measurable snow fell were southwest and west of Grand Island, with between 1 and 2 inches of accumulation on grassy areas. Elsewhere, actual air temperature and ground temperature allowed the snow to melt on contact. That area of snow moved into eastern Nebraska by sunset, but in its wake, more patchy areas of snow continued to develop  through the evening. After sunset, snow began accumulating across the rest of the area. By midnight, a fairly large but unorganized area of precipitation enveloped much of the Central Plains, extending from southern South Dakota to Oklahoma. Across south central Nebraska, the snow did fill-in after midnight, becoming steadier, but with diminishing intensity. The snow ended across the area by 8 AM Saturday. There was one isolated report of 6 inches in Minden. Otherwise the highest reports included 5.4 inches in Bradshaw, and 5.0 inches at Palmer, Elwood, and Bladen. Impacts from this snowfall were minimal as it occurred on a Friday night. Schools were not in session and no morning or evening commutes were involved. Due to the recent warm weather, snow struggled to accumulate on roads and parking lots, which also helped. The wet, heavy character of the snow coated all the trees like cotton candy and resulted in a 6-inch diameter tree branch being snapped off near Shelby. ||There was no low pressure system associated with this snowfall. Strong high pressure was over central Canada and extended southeast into the Ohio Valley. A cold front was stationary from the Gulf Coast states across Texas, with its western extent stationary, banked up against the Rockies. In the upper-levels, the flow across the U.S. was west-southwest, with a subtropical high over the Gulf of Mexico. This weak shortwave trough came out of the Desert Southwest, and it weakened further as it crossed the Central Plains. By sunset Saturday, it was a minimal dip in the 500 mb heights over the Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 5 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of high sustained winds were measured across the far western portion of the Nebraska Panhandle in Banner and Kimball Counties. Highest sustained winds measured 43 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observation station K1BW in Bravo (Pine Bluffs) measured the reported sustained winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of high sustained winds were measured across the far western portion of the Nebraska Panhandle in Banner and Kimball Counties. Highest sustained winds measured 43 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observation station K1DW Delta near Kimball measured the reported sustained wind speed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On this Thursday, the first severe thunderstorms of the year occurred, followed by windy conditions and even accumulating snow in some areas. A few weak thunderstorms began forming around 1230 PM CST, with two small clusters over Harlan and Clay counties. Other showers and thunderstorms developed south of Interstate 80, and by mid-afternoon, they had moved across and to the north of the Interstate. A couple of these storms became severe and produced large amounts of hail (the freezing level was around 8,000 ft AGL). One storm turned severe from northwest Kearney county into south-central Buffalo county, where hail up to the size of ping pong balls occurred, including in the southwest part of the city of Kearney. There was a narrow swath of Interstate 80 that was covered with hail, and snowplows needed to be deployed. The other severe storm tracked from near Edgar to McCool Junction, where it also produced hail up to the size of ping pong balls. The thunderstorm activity ended by late afternoon. These storms were elevated, on the cold side of a strong cold front. As the colder temperatures near-surface moved in, and lapse rates steepened, north winds increased to 30 to 35 mph, with frequent gusts between 45 and 55 mph. There were a few higher wind gusts, including 63 mph at the Lexington airport, which was the highest gust recorded, 59 mph near Odessa, and 58 mph at Cozad and Hastings. Late in the afternoon, the thunderstorms weakened into showers which eventually morphed into stratiform rain as they encountered stability. This rain moved into increasingly colder air where it changed to snow north of a line from Lexington to Fullerton. One to 5 inches of snow fell, with most areas measuring 1 to 3 inches. The highest amounts were 5 inches near Scotia, 4 inches near Johnson Lake, with 3 inches at Greeley and Ord. Snow began accumulating during the late afternoon, and continued through the evening hours. The snow rapidly dissipated just after midnight CST.||At sunrise, a 1000 mb low pressure system was over southeast Colorado, with a warm front extending east across northern Kansas. A stationary Arctic front was over the northern Rockies and Plains. During the day, the stationary front become mobile and accelerated southward, merging with the occluded low and its fronts. The forward momentum of the Arctic air shoved the combined front south, also forcing the Colorado low to weaken as it migrated along the front into central Kansas. In the upper-levels, the longwave pattern was typical of the mean flow that had stubbornly prevailed since the middle of November. The flow over North America was split, with a trough over the western U.S. and a ridge over the Gulf of Mexico. The flow was from the southwest over the Central Plains, with a potent, intensifying shortwave trough over Colorado and New Mexico during the morning. By sunset, a small closed low had formed along the Nebraska-Kansas border. Due to the confluence of the northern and southern streams, the low quickly dissipated and, by Friday morning, all that was left was a modest shortwave trough over Wisconsin and Illinois.

A few weak thunderstorms began forming around 1230 PM CST, with two small clusters over Harlan and Clay counties. Other showers and thunderstorms developed south of Interstate 80, and by mid-afternoon, they had moved across and to the north of the Interstate. A couple of these storms became severe and produced large amounts of hail (the freezing level was around 8,000 ft AGL). One storm turned severe from northwest Kearney county into south-central Buffalo county, where hail up to the size of ping pong balls occurred, including in the southwest part of the city of Kearney. There was a narrow swath of Interstate 80 that was covered with hail, and snowplows needed to be deployed. The other severe storm tracked from near Edgar to McCool Junction, where it also produced hail up to the size of ping pong balls. The thunderstorm activity ended by late afternoon. These storms were elevated, on the cold side of a strong cold front. As the colder temperatures near-surface moved in, and lapse rates steepened, north winds increased to 30 to 35 mph, with frequent gusts between 45 and 55 mph. There were a few higher wind gusts, including 63 mph at the Lexington airport, which was the highest gust recorded, 59 mph near Odessa, and 58 mph at Cozad and Hastings. Late in the afternoon, the thunderstorms weakened into showers which eventually morphed into stratiform rain as they encountered stability. This rain moved into increasingly colder air where it changed to snow north of a line from Lexington to Fullerton. One to 5 inches of snow fell, with most areas measuring 1 to 3 inches. The highest amounts were 5 inches near Scotia, 4 inches near Johnson Lake, with 3 inches at Greeley and Ord. Snow began accumulating during the late afternoon, and continued through the evening hours. The snow rapidly dissipated just after midnight CST.||At sunrise, a 1000 mb low pressure system was over southeast Colorado, with a warm front extending east across northern Kansas. A stationary Arctic front was over the northern Rockies and Plains. During the day, the stationary front become mobile and accelerated southward, merging with the occluded low and its fronts. The forward momentum of the Arctic air shoved the combined front south, also forcing the Colorado low to weaken as it migrated along the front into central Kansas. In the upper-levels, the longwave pattern was typical of the mean flow that had stubbornly prevailed since the middle of November. The flow over North America was split, with a trough over the western U.S. and a ridge over the Gulf of Mexico. The flow was from the southwest over the Central Plains, with a potent, intensifying shortwave trough over Colorado and New Mexico during the morning. By sunset, a small closed low had formed along the Nebraska-Kansas border. Due to the confluence of the northern and southern streams, the low quickly dissipated and, by Friday morning, all that was left was a modest shortwave trough over Wisconsin and Illinois.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On this Thursday, the first severe thunderstorms of the year occurred, followed by windy conditions and even accumulating snow in some areas. A few weak thunderstorms began forming around 1230 PM CST, with two small clusters over Harlan and Clay counties. Other showers and thunderstorms developed south of Interstate 80, and by mid-afternoon, they had moved across and to the north of the Interstate. A couple of these storms became severe and produced large amounts of hail (the freezing level was around 8,000 ft AGL). One storm turned severe from northwest Kearney county into south-central Buffalo county, where hail up to the size of ping pong balls occurred, including in the southwest part of the city of Kearney. There was a narrow swath of Interstate 80 that was covered with hail, and snowplows needed to be deployed. The other severe storm tracked from near Edgar to McCool Junction, where it also produced hail up to the size of ping pong balls. The thunderstorm activity ended by late afternoon. These storms were elevated, on the cold side of a strong cold front. As the colder temperatures near-surface moved in, and lapse rates steepened, north winds increased to 30 to 35 mph, with frequent gusts between 45 and 55 mph. There were a few higher wind gusts, including 63 mph at the Lexington airport, which was the highest gust recorded, 59 mph near Odessa, and 58 mph at Cozad and Hastings. Late in the afternoon, the thunderstorms weakened into showers which eventually morphed into stratiform rain as they encountered stability. This rain moved into increasingly colder air where it changed to snow north of a line from Lexington to Fullerton. One to 5 inches of snow fell, with most areas measuring 1 to 3 inches. The highest amounts were 5 inches near Scotia, 4 inches near Johnson Lake, with 3 inches at Greeley and Ord. Snow began accumulating during the late afternoon, and continued through the evening hours. The snow rapidly dissipated just after midnight CST.||At sunrise, a 1000 mb low pressure system was over southeast Colorado, with a warm front extending east across northern Kansas. A stationary Arctic front was over the northern Rockies and Plains. During the day, the stationary front become mobile and accelerated southward, merging with the occluded low and its fronts. The forward momentum of the Arctic air shoved the combined front south, also forcing the Colorado low to weaken as it migrated along the front into central Kansas. In the upper-levels, the longwave pattern was typical of the mean flow that had stubbornly prevailed since the middle of November. The flow over North America was split, with a trough over the western U.S. and a ridge over the Gulf of Mexico. The flow was from the southwest over the Central Plains, with a potent, intensifying shortwave trough over Colorado and New Mexico during the morning. By sunset, a small closed low had formed along the Nebraska-Kansas border. Due to the confluence of the northern and southern streams, the low quickly dissipated and, by Friday morning, all that was left was a modest shortwave trough over Wisconsin and Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 1 to 4 inches across the county. The combination of snow and strong winds resulted in visibility dropping down to less than 1/2 mile at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early morning heavy banded snowfall moved across the central and northeast portions of the Nebraska Panhandle producing high snowfall rates of up to 6 inches per hour based on overnight storm reports. By morning observations of various CoCoRaHS, COOP observers, law enforcement and public many county areas were blanketed with up to a foot of snow creating travel impacts as many roads were impassible while waiting for crews to clear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer at station NE-DS-1 located 11.6 miles south of Chadron or two miles southeast of Chadron State Park measured a storm total snowfall of 7.5 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Official in Hemingford measured snowfall total of 6.0 inches with noted travel impacts as United States Highway 385 was completely snow-covered.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system caused winter storm conditions to develop during the late morning hours on March 19th, which continued into the early morning on March 20th. Total snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 8 inches. The snow combined with northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts around 45 mph at times to produce blowing and drifting snow with visibility as low as a quarter mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: General public located near Merritt Reservoir reported 7 inches of snowfall. Snowfall amounts of 4 to 8 inches were common across the southeastern half of western Cherry County. North winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph caused visibility of 1/4 mile at times with blowing and drifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent rainfall from a potent, slow moving upper low pressure system led to heavy rain and isolated flooding across south central Nebraska heading into the Memorial Day Holiday weekend. The worst of the flooding occurred near the Crystal Lake Recreation Area in Adams County, but pockets of minor/brief flooding were noted in and around Kearney as well.||Rain first moved into the area during the early morning hours of Thursday, May 21st as a closed upper low pressure system slowly moved into the area. More persistent and heavier rain developed within a west to east band focused along the Highway 6 and Interstate 80 corridors around midday Thursday, and persisted through the late afternoon. Brief and minor flooding occurred in poor drainage areas in and around Kearney Thursday afternoon. Activity weakened and became somewhat disorganized during the evening hours, but pockets of light to moderate rain persisted through Thursday night and into Friday morning, May 22nd. The most persistent band of rain, with some embedded thunderstorms, developed around dawn on Friday and blossomed in coverage such that the entire area was seeing rainfall by noon as the upper low slowly inched its way into north central Kansas. Rainfall was efficient through the entire afternoon due to a persistent feed of moisture into the system from the southeast and very slow movement of the upper low. This led to increased runoff into area streams and rivers such that sandbagging was needed by Friday evening in Holstein due to rising river levels along the Little Blue River. Rainfall rates slowly decreased Friday evening and the upper low gained a little bit of eastward momentum as it moved into southeast Nebraska and northeast Kansas. New thunderstorm development in these areas began to cut off the moisture flow into south central Nebraska, with only isolated light showers persisting into Friday night. Widespread rain amounts of 3 to 6 inches resulted in flooding along the Little Blue River, as well as the Crystal Lake Recreation Area, which led to camper evacuations. Flood damages and lost revenue totaled $10,000 for the recreation area alone. Additional damage occurred to infrastructure including Highway 74 near Ayr (which was closed for a time on Saturday, May 23rd), numerous county roads, as well as a BNSF railroad bridge just north of Crystal Lake.||Meteorologically speaking, it was somewhat unusual to have such a potent, slow-moving, and cut-off upper low pressure system so late into spring. The slow moving nature of the upper low, along with nearly stationary forcing from deformation within the northwest quadrant, were the key factors that led to such heavy rain amounts as precipitable water values were not overly high, around 1.1��� to 1.4���. Persistent cloud cover, cool surface temperatures, and weak mid level lapse rates kept instability values seasonably low, generally less than 1000 J/kg, meaning this heavy rain event was largely synoptic driven, with a lesser convective component than normally seen during late May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain around and upstream of this area resulted in flooding during the Memorial Day holiday weekend. Widespread amounts of 3 to 6 inches fell, with much of the flooding affecting areas along the Little Blue River. Sandbagging was required in Holstein on the evening of the 22nd due to rising waters. Flooding affected the Crystal Lake Recreation Area near Ayr. All campers were evacuated, with flooding causing damage and a loss of revenue for the holiday weekend, totaling approximately 10,000 dollars. Numerous rural roads were damaged, and Highway 74 near Ayr was closed for a period of time due to debris becoming jammed under a bridge, causing water to rise over the road. The highway reopened during the late evening hours of May 23rd. Major damage to a BNSF trestle bridge was reported north of the Crystal Lake campground due to clogged water drainage and undermining. Flooding subsided on the 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold surface high pressure settled over western and north central Nebraska during the early morning and morning hours on May 10th. This caused freezing temperatures in the 20s across much of the area. The coldest temperatures was 21 degrees at Gordon airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gordon AWOS recorded a minimum temperature of 21 degrees. Low temperatures in the lower to mid 20s were common throughout Sheridan County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The late evening of Monday the 24th into the morning of Tuesday the 25th featured one of the most intriguing snow events of the 2019-20 winter season within South Central Nebraska. While the majority of the 24-county area (including the Tri Cities) received little to no overnight accumulation, an intense/narrow mesoscale band dumped as much as 6-10 of wet snow on portions of Dawson, Gosper, Furnas and southwestern Harlan counties. Amounts this high concentrated within a swath no more than 10-15 miles wide, with totals tapering off to barely a dusting only a few miles on either side. Per several official reports (NWS coop observer/CoCoRaHS) and a few unofficial ones, some of the highest totals featured: 10 in Elwood, 9 nine miles north of Edison, 8 in Edison, 6-8 at Beaver City and 7.1 six miles southwest of Lexington. On the periphery of the main band, more modest amounts included: 4.5 in Cozad, 4 at Gothenburg and 3 in Cambridge. Despite the hefty totals within the primary snow stripe, the fact that this was a late night/early morning event and that winds averaged only 10-15 MPH through most of it kept travel impacts somewhat in check through roughly sunrise. However, north winds picked up as the day of the 25th wore on, with frequent gusts of 25-35 MPH combining with residual light snowfall to promote some drifting and reduced visibility. ||Breaking down timing, the main event associated with the intense mesoscale band unfolded over primarily a 7-9 hour span, getting underway between 10 p.m. and midnight as spotty light rain transitioned to steady snow, and weakening/gradually sinking southward into Kansas between 5-7 a.m. In the mid-upper levels, the primary player was a potent shortwave trough diving into the Central Plains from the north-northwest, featuring a compact/intense closed circulation that peaked in intensity while traversing the local area (especially evident at 700 millibars). As the already-strong zone of deep convergence/frontogenesis intercepted a pocket of steep mid-level lapse rates, the pronounced mesoscale band blossomed, starting out oriented predominantly north-south but gradually pivoting more northwest-southeast in response to the movement of the parent system. At the surface, the relatively light breezes through the night resulted from the region temporarily residing within the heart of an inverted trough axis, before this boundary slipped south around sunrise and allowed stronger northerly winds to kick in. Temperature-wise, the wet nature of snow resulted from overnight readings holding up mainly in the low 30s F. From a forecast perspective this was a challenging event, as although it became increasingly evident 24-48 hours out that mesoscale banding might ensue, its eventual placement/intensity remained a major question mark until it materialized on radar. In the end, although forecasted maximum snow amounts proved to be significantly under-done, a good chance for at least 2-4 within the broader general area became apparent by the afternoon of the 24th, prompting the issuance of a Winter Weather Advisory.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall reports generally ranged from an inch or 2 across the eastern half of the county, with higher amounts of 4-8 inches across the western half of the county. Reported totals included 7.1 inches six miles southwest of Lexington, 6.1 inches four miles south of Cozad, 4.5 inches in Cozad and 4 inches at Gothenburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level short wave came off of the Rocky Mountains out over the central Plains. This led to a surface low developing over Kansas. In front of that surface low, warm moist air was advected north. Isentropic upglide and led to an overrunning precipitation event that led to some high snowfall totals in eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa. It was the #5 (5.4 inches) and #4 (4.5 inches) highest April snowfall events for Omaha and Lincoln respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 8 inches of snow at Schuyler, NE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Stubborn cold air remained near the surface while strong warm advection ahead of a mid-level trough resulted in a distinct above-freezing layer. The result was a period of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle, which produced a glaze of ice on objects and roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of light freezing rain resulted in ice accumulation from one to two tenths of an inch, followed by light snowfall with up to 0.6 inches at Emerson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most significant winter storm during January started as a surge of warm advection, followed by a strong trough through the southern Rockies on Friday, January 17. A period of moderate to heavy snowfall swept across the area, accompanied by southerly winds gusting to 35 to 45 mph. Snowfall then tapered off to a period of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle into the evening hours, before changing back to snow. By late evening and early in the overnight, winds transitioned to northwest and increased to 35 to 45 mph, with gusts of 50 to 60 mph common as low pressure intensified across Iowa. Strong winds persisted most of Saturday as the area of low pressure moved slowly toward the western Great Lakes. Most areas received between 4 and 8 inches of snowfall as well as a glaze of ice, with just shy of a quarter inch of ice in the Sioux City area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of heavy snowfall with winds gusting to 30 to 40 mph moved through during the morning and early afternoon of January 17, before tapering to several hours of freezing drizzle. Ice accumulations were generally a glaze to a tenth of an inch, before precipitation changed back to snow in the evening. Winds then picked up from the northwest, with peak wind gusts during the morning reaching 47 mph 2 miles east of Concord. Combined with snowfall and previous snow, winds produced numerous hours with visibility of around one-quarter mile or less with travel not recommended. Total snowfall reached 5.5 inches at Newcastle and Ponca, and 4.4 inches at Concord.

Combined with snowfall and previous snow, winds produced numerous hours with visibility of around one-quarter mile or less with travel not recommended. Total snowfall reached 2.2 inches at Emerson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorm developed in the northern Nebraska panhandle the afternoon of 2 July 2020. This supercell produced very large hail and a tornado touched down near Marsland Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS damage survey confirmed EF-2 Tornado that developed 5.8 miles east of Marsland and traveled west-northwest for 3.4 miles. Several large hardwood tree trunks/branches snapped. Trees uprooted, center pivot point mangled and three steel power poles were destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level disturbance worked northeast from Colorado early morning January 12 into Iowa by late day.  Broad lift initially within the warm advection zone became increasingly concentrated, with deeper lift in the dendritic growth layer by afternoon across northwest Iowa.  Snowfall rates up to 1.5 to 2 inches per hour were noted across northwest Iowa and northeast Nebraska at the peak during the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to briefly heavy snowfall during the afternoon accumulated to 5.0 inches at Ponca and 3.3 inches near Concord, resulting in hazardous road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A light freezing rain and dense freezing fog event occurred between the 16th and 17th of January in southwest Nebraska. Ice accumulations and poor visibility led to dangerous driving conditions across the area. Although numerous minor car accidents were reported in McCook, roads largely remained open.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The McCook Police Department reported that light freezing rain and dense freezing fog had led to icy road surfaces which caused numerous minor accidents across the city of McCook throughout the day on January 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Brief evening period of high winds were felt across the far southwestern portion of the Nebraska Panhandle with maximum winds gusts of 59 mph being measured out in Scotts Bluff and Kimball.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sensor at Scotts Bluff or two miles west of Gering measured the reported wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Brief evening period of high winds were felt across the far southwestern portion of the Nebraska Panhandle with maximum winds gusts of 59 mph being measured out in Scotts Bluff and Kimball.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nebraska Department of Roads sensor NE001 along Banner Road on North 71 near Long Springs measured the period of sustained winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Brief evening period of high winds were felt across the far southwestern portion of the Nebraska Panhandle with maximum winds gusts of 59 mph being measured out in Scotts Bluff and Kimball.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust was measured at the Kimball Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of heavy snowfall fell across portions of the southern Nebraska Panhandle. COOP stations near Broadwater and south of Bushnell measured heavy snowfall amounts of 6.0 and 7.1 inches respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer located fifteen miles south of Bushnell measured a total snowfall of 7.1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of heavy snowfall fell across portions of the southern Nebraska Panhandle. COOP stations near Broadwater and south of Bushnell measured heavy snowfall amounts of 6.0 and 7.1 inches respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer located ten miles south-southeast of Broadwater measured a total snowfall of 6.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Brisk winds in advance of the center of strong arctic high pressure brought wind chill readings to -25 to -35 during the overnight and morning of January 19.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coldest wind chill readings reached -30F at 0300CST January 19 at a location 2 miles east of Concord.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought heavy rain and a landspout to portions of south central Nebraska the evening of Sunday, May 24 into the early morning hours of Memorial Day, Monday, May 25.||Just after 6 PM CDT Sunday evening, weak thundershowers began developing along a frontal boundary that was located from near Stockton, KS to Red Cloud, NE to Shelby, NE. One of these weak thundershowers managed to spawn a landspout tornado a few miles west of Davenport, NE, in extreme northeastern portions of Nuckolls County. This landspout, unlike most, did cause some damage as nearly a dozen empty coal railroad cars were overturned. The aforementioned line of thunderstorms strengthened slightly during the evening, per radar reflectivity, before weakening with loss of daytime heating around sunset. The landspout was the only known severe weather caused by this line of thunderstorms as the main focus for severe convection remained further south across Kansas near the Interstate 70 corridor.||Showers and thunderstorms blossomed over south central Nebraska and north central Kansas after 10 PM Sunday and continued through the entire overnight. This activity remained non-severe, but very slow storm motions and abundant deep layer moisture allowed for heavy rain rates and, subsequently, pockets of very heavy rainfall. The heaviest rainfall amounts of 4 to 8 inches (per radar and ground truth observations) appeared to focus across northwest Adams County and southwest Hall County. Numerous county roads became submerged in water and impassable late Sunday night into early Monday morning. The most severe impact, however, was to a residence northwest of Kenesaw as flood water filled the basement and even encroached on the main floor, prompting their rescue early Monday morning. The rescue had to be done via air boat because so many roads in the area were under water and impassable. ||The heavy rain from Sunday night into Monday morning occurred over areas that were already saturated from recent heavy rains from May 21-23, and this likely contributed to the flooding impacts from this event. Very slow storm motions and seasonably high precipitable water values near 1.5��� were also meteorological factors to this heavy rain event. The tornado occurred in conditions fairly typical of landspouts as it was along a surface front with abundant ambient vorticity that was stretched into the vertical axis by steep low level lapse rates and sufficient 0-3 km CAPE near 100 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout tornado was confirmed via several eyewitness reports and a damage survey in northern Nuckolls County. This tornado formed along a weak frontal boundary and exhibited the classic landspout characteristics of a long, translucent condensation/dust funnel. The landspout moved southwest along the boundary for over 3 miles. The main damage along its path was the overturning of 11 empty coal railroad cars. Otherwise, damage was minimal. The maximum wind speed was estimated to be 80 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system (MCS) developed over central Nebraska, then moved east-southeast during the early morning hours of Sunday, May 24, bringing damaging winds to portions of the area.||Thunderstorms developed just west of the Hastings County Warning Area (CWA), in Lincoln and Custer Counties, around 10 PM CDT on the evening of Saturday, May 23. Another line of thunderstorms developed over western Nebraska and moved eastward through the evening hours. The central Nebraska cluster was very slow moving, and even stationary at times, which allowed the two clusters to merge over Custer County around midnight on Sunday, May 24. Shortly after this merger, the line of thunderstorms gained eastward momentum and moved into Valley and Sherman Counties around 1 AM CDT, bringing with them strong, damaging winds. The line weakened slightly as it continued to track east-southeast during the early morning hours on Sunday, with the strongest activity tracking along and northeast of a line from Loup City, NE to York, NE. Measured wind gusts were generally near or less than 60 mph, including a gust of 54 mph measured by the ASOS at the Ord Airport, and a 57mph gust measured by a mesonet site at Scotia. The strongest measured gust was 63mph by a mesonet station 6 NNE of Sherman Reservoir at 1:35 AM CDT. Estimated wind gusts as high as 70 mph were reported in Valley and Sherman Counties, which resulted in the toppling of several irrigation pivots in these areas. The line of thunderstorms exited the Hastings CWA to the east between 4-5 AM CDT.||A nocturnal line of thunderstorms (aka MCS), such as this, is common for this area during the late spring and summer months. Thunderstorms were fueled by a 40 kt southerly low level jet, which advected strong instability (most unstable CAPE values near 3000 J/kg) from northern Kansas into central Nebraska and provided convergence/ascent for elevated parcels at the terminus of this jet over central Nebraska. Moderate effective shear up to 40 kt also aided in thunderstorm organization, along with an established cold pool associated with the western cluster of convection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was a fairly windy Tuesday across all of South Central Nebraska, especially between late-morning and sunset, with north-northwest winds commonly sustained 20-35 MPH and frequent gusts 35-45 MPH behind a typical spring cold front. However, primarily between 3-9 p.m. CDT a few bands of high-based rain showers and a few embedded weak thunderstorms passed through the area, allowing more intense gusts of 55-60+ MPH to mix down to the surface along various gust fronts. A few of the strongest official severe-criteria gusts at area airports included 61 MPH at Ord and 59 MPH at Kearney. Meanwhile, unofficial personal weather station clocked gusts up to 63 MPH near Fullerton and 62 MPH near Bladen. There was no known damage from these locally-enhanced winds, but there were several reports (and social media posts) of blowing dust. ||In the mid-upper levels, the strong winds and passing showers were instigated by a potent shortwave trough diving southeastward into the Central Plains. Deep mixing and fairly strong, unidirectional wind profiles from the surface to the mid-levels were present, but ambient wind speeds at the 850 millibar level only averaged 30-40 knots. Thus, it reasons that without the presence of shallow convection, sporadic severe-criteria winds would not have mixed to the surface. However, the low-level environment ahead of the weak convection was supportive of strong downdrafts, featuring steep lapse rates and pronounced surface temperature-dewpoint spreads on the order of 25-30 F, as afternoon highs topped out mainly low-mid 70s F with dewpoints averaging low-40s F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 59 MPH was measured by the Kearney Regional Airport AWOS.

The tornado occurred in conditions fairly typical of landspouts as it was along a surface front with abundant ambient vorticity that was stretched into the vertical axis by steep low level lapse rates and sufficient 0-3 km CAPE near 100 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Radar estimates depicted an area of approximately 4 to 8 inches of rain fell in a very concentrated area across far southwestern portions of Hall County and far northwestern portions of Adams County. Numerous rural roads were covered in water and impassable for a period of time. At least 19 roads were marked with barricades and at least 1 culvert was lost. A number of roads were washed out pretty well, materials like dirt, crushed concrete and gravel were needed for repairs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified moving from southeast Colorado on the morning of March 19, into the Great Lakes early on March 20. A strong band of mid-level frontogenesis progressed slowly eastward across the area during the afternoon and evening hours. The system was initially quite warm, resulting in a swath of moderate to heavy rainfall from northeast Nebraska into northwest Iowa. As the system strengthened on the 19th, much colder air was dragged into the frontal band, and precipitation turned quickly from rain to snow from northwest to southeast, and was accompanied by winds gusting 40 to 55 mph. Snowfall was generally from 1 to 5 inches near and south of Interstate 90, with heaviest bands near the Missouri River and again from the Sioux Falls area eastward along Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed quickly to snowfall by early evening on March 19, with visibility to one-half mile at times between 1700-2100CST. The intense snowfall rate made travel hazardous during the evening as winds increased to a peak of 41 mph at Yankton and 38 mph at Wayne. Snowfall totaled 5.4 inches at Concord.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of May 4th, several severe thunderstorms moved eastward from Colorado in the southwestern Nebraska area. This episode saw reports of damaging thunderstorm wind gusts up to 80 mph in addition to hail up to 1 in size and rainfall totals up to 1 in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 1 inch hail at his location via a phone call.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although not overly-impactful to area travel, Thursday the 16th featured a fairly noteworthy late-season snowfall across the majority of South Central Nebraska (this was the final snow event of the 2019-20 season). Most counties along and north of the Highway 6 corridor measured somewhere between 2-5 of wet snow, with the highest concentration of 4 to localized 6 amounts concentrated within northeastern counties including: Greeley, Nance, Merrick, Hamilton, Polk and York. Snow accumulated most efficiently on grassy and elevated surfaces, while much of it melted/accumulated minimally on area roads due to marginal, near-freezing temperatures. Per NWS coop observers, some of the highest totals featured: 5.9 north of York, 5.5 in Greeley, 5.3 in Osceola and 5.1 at Bradshaw. In the Tri Cities, official amounts ranged from 2.4 at Hastings to 3.8 at Grand Island. At the York and Greeley NWS observer sites, the aforementioned daily snow totals over 5 were the highest on record for so late in the spring! EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although not overly-impactful to area travel, Thursday the 16th featured a fairly noteworthy late-season snowfall across the majority of South Central Nebraska (this was the final snow event of the 2019-20 season). Most counties along and north of the Highway 6 corridor measured somewhere between 2-5 of wet snow, with the highest concentration of 4 to localized 6 amounts concentrated within northeastern counties including: Greeley, Nance, Merrick, Hamilton, Polk and York. Snow accumulated most efficiently on grassy and elevated surfaces, while much of it melted/accumulated minimally on area roads due to marginal, near-freezing temperatures. Per NWS coop observers, some of the highest totals featured: 5.9 north of York, 5.5 in Greeley, 5.3 in Osceola and 5.1 at Bradshaw. In the Tri Cities, official amounts ranged from 2.4 at Hastings to 3.8 at Grand Island. At the York and Greeley NWS observer sites, the aforementioned daily snow totals over 5 were the highest on record for so late in the spring! At Grand Island and Hastings, this was the 6th-highest and 8th-highest daily snowfall this late in the spring, respectively. Thanks mainly to the wet nature of snow, drifting was fairly minimal, despite steady northeast winds sustained around 20 MPH/gusts around 30 MPH. Although the sight of fresh snow cover was rather unusual for mid-April, it actually turned into a distant memory quite quickly, melting rapidly on Friday the 17th thanks to high temperatures reaching the low 50s F.  ||Digging into event timing, snow broke out across most of the area between 8-10 a.m. CDT, peaked in intensity late-morning through late-afternoon, and gradually departed from west to east during the evening with the vast majority of locations snow-free by 10 p.m. CDT. In the mid-upper levels, the primary player was strong upper level divergence and resultant mid level frontogenesis (quite pronounced at 700 millibars), focused slightly downstream from a positively-tilted parent trough axis positioned over the northern Great Basin and Central Rockies. At the surface, the steady (but not overly-strong) northeast winds were driven by a modest pressure gradient between deepening low pressure centered over the northern Texas Panhandle and broad high pressure stretched from the Northern Rockies into the Midwest. Temperature-wise, the 16th was incredibly cold by mid-April standards, featuring daytime highs of only 32-35 F across most of South Central Nebraska. In fact, this snow was the precipitation highlight of a markedly-cold, six-day stretch centered from the 12th-17th. At Hastings airport, the average daily temperature over these six days of 34.4 F (16 degrees below normal) established a record for the overall-coldest six-day stretch so late in the spring.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 4 inches across the county, with the highest totals occurring across far north-northeastern portions of the county. A total of 2.4 inches was reported from the cooperative observer located 6 miles east-southeast of Clay Center.  A CoCoRaHS observer from 5 miles south-southeast of Clay Center reported 2.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As mid-April Easter Sundays go, this one likely ranked as one of the overall-crummiest weather-wise in South Central Nebraska. While the entire area had relentless north winds and very cold temperatures, many places also experienced several hours of freezing drizzle and at least brief snow. In addition to (and resulting from) this combination of weather elements, several counties also endured fairly widespread power outages, both of the brief/intermittent and multiple-hour variety. These power-related issues mainly focused within a southwest-northeast swath through the heart of the area (including counties such as Franklin/Adams/Hall/Polk) as the nasty combination of strong winds and steady freezing drizzle coated power lines in ice, promoting pronounced line galloping and resultant outages/infrastructure damage. As just a few examples: the Grand Island Utilities Director called this one of the top-five worst power outage events of his career; and the Polk County Rural Power District reported at least 25 broken poles. Getting back to the weather itself, the entire day featured strong north winds, frequently gusting 40-50+ MPH. While peak gusts at official airport stations topped out slightly below severe-criteria, a few personal weather stations clocked 58 MPH, including one south of Kearney and another in Nelson. The vast majority of wintry precipitation occurred during the daytime hours, largely ending well-before sunset. Light-but-persistent freezing drizzle was the main issue, especially within the aforementioned, southwest-northeast corridor of counties that experienced the worst power outages. Most ice accretion targeted elevated surfaces (e.g. power lines), with only minimal road impacts given near-freezing surface temperatures. As for snow, the vast majority of the area only accumulated a light dusting of no more than one-quarter to one-half inch, although much of it fell in a short-but-intense burst as a narrow band swung through from west-to-east, briefly reducing visibility to as low as one-quarter mile. The main concentration of slightly higher snow amounts in the 1-2 range targeted Valley County in the far northern local area, as any truly-significant snow totals remained north-through-northwest of South Central Nebraska. Temperature-wise, the 12th was impressively-cold by mid-April standards, featuring daytime highs of only 31-35 F across most of the area (roughly 30 degrees below normal) and wind chill values only in the teens. In fact, this event ushered in a markedly-cold, six-day stretch centered from April 12th-17th. At Hastings airport, the average daily temperature over these six days of 34.4 F (16 degrees below normal) established a record for the overall-coldest six-day stretch so late in the spring.||Finishing up with the meteorological background behind this unpleasant Easter, the main player in the mid-upper levels was a large-scale trough traversing the central CONUS, highlighted by broad low pressure and various embedded disturbances extending southward from southern Canada, and also a potent southern stream shortwave trough ejecting east-northeast across Texas. Given that South Central Nebraska resided just south of the main northern stream disturbance, mid-level saturation ended up lacking somewhat, thus promoting more drizzle than snow overall. At the surface, a powerful cold front blasted through the local area during the pre-dawn hours of the 12th, turning the pleasant temperatures in the 70s F on the 11th into a fleeting memory. The intense north winds were driven by a stout pressure gradient between a roughly 997 millibar low tracking east across the Southern Plains and a 1032 millibar high emanating southeastward from the Northern Rockies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of an extended period of light freezing precipitation and frequent gusts over 50 MPH resulted in power outages across the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of an extended period of light freezing precipitation and frequent gusts over 50 MPH resulted in power outages across the area. The Grand Island Utilities Director called this one of the top-five worst power outage events of his career.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As mid-April Easter Sundays go, this one likely ranked as one of the overall-crummiest weather-wise in South Central Nebraska. While the entire area had relentless north winds and very cold temperatures, many places also experienced several hours of freezing drizzle and at least brief snow. In addition to (and resulting from) this combination of weather elements, several counties also endured fairly widespread power outages, both of the brief/intermittent and multiple-hour variety. These power-related issues mainly focused within a southwest-northeast swath through the heart of the area (including counties such as Franklin/Adams/Hall/Polk) as the nasty combination of strong winds and steady freezing drizzle coated power lines in ice, promoting pronounced line galloping and resultant outages/infrastructure damage. As just a few examples: the Grand Island Utilities Director called this one of the top-five worst power outage events of his career; and the Polk County Rural Power District reported at least 25 broken poles. Getting back to the weather itself, the entire day featured strong north winds, frequently gusting 40-50+ MPH. While peak gusts at official airport stations topped out slightly below severe-criteria, a few personal weather stations clocked 58 MPH, including one south of Kearney and another in Nelson. The vast majority of wintry precipitation occurred during the daytime hours, largely ending well-before sunset. Light-but-persistent freezing drizzle was the main issue, especially within the aforementioned, southwest-northeast corridor of counties that experienced the worst power outages. Most ice accretion targeted elevated surfaces (e.g. power lines), with only minimal road impacts given near-freezing surface temperatures. As for snow, the vast majority of the area only accumulated a light dusting of no more than one-quarter to one-half inch, although much of it fell in a short-but-intense burst as a narrow band swung through from west-to-east, briefly reducing visibility to as low as one-quarter mile. The main concentration of slightly higher snow amounts in the 1-2 range targeted Valley County in the far northern local area, as any truly-significant snow totals remained north-through-northwest of South Central Nebraska. Temperature-wise, the 12th was impressively-cold by mid-April standards, featuring daytime highs of only 31-35 F across most of the area (roughly 30 degrees below normal) and wind chill values only in the teens. In fact, this event ushered in a markedly-cold, six-day stretch centered from April 12th-17th. At Hastings airport, the average daily temperature over these six days of 34.4 F (16 degrees below normal) established a record for the overall-coldest six-day stretch so late in the spring.||Finishing up with the meteorological background behind this unpleasant Easter, the main player in the mid-upper levels was a large-scale trough traversing the central CONUS, highlighted by broad low pressure and various embedded disturbances extending southward from southern Canada, and also a potent southern stream shortwave trough ejecting east-northeast across Texas. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front associated with a strong trough digging into the Northern Plains brought a period of very strong winds, gusting as high as 40 to 50 mph, with a period of light snowfall. While accumulations were generally less than an inch, the areas with heavier snow from February 8-9 experienced an extended period of ground blizzard conditions from east central South Dakota into southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting to 35 to 45 mph at South Sioux City, when combined with new snowfall of less than one-half inch, produced visibility briefly less than one mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow was measured in portions of Sioux County on the third and fourth. A public report in Harrison had a heavy snowfall measurement of 6.8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of 6.8 inches of heavy snow in Harrison Nebraska.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam occurred on the Platte River near Fremont. This resulted in minor flooding and at least 1 water evacuation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Voluntary evacuation ordered for Big Island Road. Flooding also occurring on Ridge Road, Ridgeland Ave, and in Hormel Park. One water rescue from a home on County Road 19. An ice jam in the Platte River caused the minor flooding. Two individuals were taken to the hospital, one with minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent snow fell across Dawes County as a front stalled over the northern Nebraska Panhandle. Heavy snowfall report of 10.0 inches was reported in Chadron.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KCSR radio station in Chadron reported 7.0 inches of snow at this time. Another report of 10.0 inches received at 11 AM on the 17th in Chadron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued strong wind and plummeting temperatures brought wind chills to 25 below to 35 below zero during the overnight and early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dipped as low as 28 below zero at South Sioux City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Confluent flow aloft resulted in a strong and narrow frontal band of snowfall from northern Nebraska into northwest Iowa. A swath of three to six inches of snowfall occurred in the heart of the banding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.6 inches at Concord and 3.2 inches at Newcastle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Confluent flow aloft resulted in a strong and narrow frontal band of snowfall from northern Nebraska into northwest Iowa. A swath of three to six inches of snowfall occurred in the heart of the banding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.1 inches at Emerson.  Visibility was briefly one-quarter to one-half mile during heaviest snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued strong wind and plummeting temperatures brought wind chills to 25 below to 35 below zero during the overnight and early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dipped as low as 28 below zero at South Sioux City and 29 below zero at Wayne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread winter weather across the Nebraska Panhandle on the 24th and 25th with enhanced snowfall amounts over the Pine Ridge south of Chadron.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer located two miles southeast of Chadron State Park along the Pine Ridge reported 6.0 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms early in the evening produced large hail and landspout tornadoes across parts of far southwest Nebraska and the northern Sandhills. The cells eventually merged into a linear system, resulting in more widespread wind damage in north central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter measured a 67 mph wind gust south of North Platte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two primary supercells moved southeast from Cherry County and vicinity toward Custer County during the late morning and afternoon. An additional round of storms consisting of a linear system and a supercell moved through north central Nebraska later in the evening. Wind gusts were estimated up to 100 mph and hail to tennis ball size, while multiple reports of damaged crops and flooded buildings were received during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of high winds and hail resulted in extensive crop damage. Wind gusts were estimated at least 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An initially unimpressive thunderstorm complex over northeast Nebraska rapidly intensified during the morning of August 10, 2020. After a few sporadic early morning damage reports, the intensifying storms began to more readily produce damaging winds in excess of 50 kts around 1340Z. Those damaging winds increased in coverage and intensity as the storms moved east into Iowa. The portion of the thunderstorm complex that moved through the NWS Omaha forecast area marked the approximate beginning of an intense derecho that tracked across portions of Iowa, Illinois, Wisconsin, Indiana, Michigan, and Ohio. Within the NWS Omaha forecast area, there were more than 20 local storm reports of wind damage and measured severe wind gusts, including more than 50,000 power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Personal weather station measured a wind of 54 kts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed on the nose of the elevated instability gradient in the late night and early morning hours. With up to 1000 J/kg MUCAPE, a few storms produced large hail from northeast Nebraska into northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was found and measured three hours after occurrence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed on the nose of the elevated instability gradient in the late night and early morning hours. With up to 1000 J/kg MUCAPE, a few storms produced large hail from northeast Nebraska into northwest Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold front brings slow moving thunderstorms and heavy rain to extreme southern portions of south central Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening of July 26. Thunderstorms developed between 3pm and 4pm CDT from southwest to northeast, generally along a line from Rooks County in north central Kansas, to Thayer County in south central Nebraska. Once thunderstorms developed, heavy rain quickly became the main concern, though a few non-severe wind gusts around 50 to 55 mph were reported across north central Kansas early on in the event, as well as an estimated wind gust near 60mph in Thayer County. The heavy rain threat was driven by several factors, including: weak mid and upper level winds which resulted in slow storm motion, training of thunderstorms over the same areas for several hours, and unusually high atmospheric moisture levels. In fact, precipitable water values were near 2��� on this evening, which is near 3 standard deviations above normal and in the upper 90th percentile of climatology. Heavy rain rates of 1-2��� per hour, or higher, were common under the strongest thunderstorm cores, especially between the hours of 4pm and 9pm CDT. Thunderstorms gradually decreased in intensity during the late evening as the persistent convection over the same areas and loss of daytime heating led to decreasing instability. The majority of the lingering light rain ended by midnight.||For south central Nebraska, the heavy rain was limited to primarily eastern Nuckolls County and all of Thayer County. Storm total rain amounts by the morning of July 27th of 3-4��� inches were common, with an isolated maximum of 4-5��� plus reported in the Hebron area. The highest reported rain amount was 5.50��� measured at the fire station in Hebron and relayed by emergency management. The primary impacts from the heavy rain were flooded roads across Thayer County. This included numerous county roads along with Highway 5 north of Deshler and Highway 81 (which is a major four lane highway) between Belvidere and the Nebraska/Kansas state line. The flooding on Highway 81 may have contributed to a semi tractor trailer to lose control and drive into a ditch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24-hour total rainfall amount of 5.15 inches was recorded by a CoCoRaHS Observer located 1 mile east-northeast of Hebron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving upper low brings multiple rounds of thunderstorms, including locally heavy rain and a few severe thunderstorms, on July 29th. Low to mid level warm air advection ahead of a lead shortwave trough led to the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms roughly along a line from Hastings to Superior between 6-8am CDT. Locally heavy rain rates and slow moving thunderstorms led to areas of heavy rain across portions of Clay, Nuckolls, Fillmore and Thayer Counties during the mid to late morning hours. Soil conditions in these areas were already saturated due to previous heavy rain events over the recent days. Activity decreased in coverage and intensity around midday, and most areas were dry by 1pm CDT. Total rain amounts of 2-3 inches were common across these areas, which led to renewed flooding, particularly over Thayer County. The primary impact was water covering county roads and water flowing over Highway 136 west of Deshler. Conditions were favorable for heavy rain given the aforementioned slow storm motions, but also relatively high precipitable water values near 2���.||Additional thunderstorm activity developed during the afternoon hours from central Nebraska into northwest Kansas. Initial individual and cellular thunderstorms grew upscale into several clusters during the late afternoon and into the evening. One such cluster moved through far western portions of the County Warning Area (CWA), mainly from Cozad down to Beaver City, between 6-8pm CDT. Some of this activity became severe and produced up to ping pong ball sized hail across Furnas County. As this activity weakened around 8pm CDT, new, mainly isolated, thunderstorms developed just to the NE over Phelps and Buffalo Counties between 8-9pm CDT. Up to penny size hail was reported with this activity over Phelps County. Yet another round of thunderstorms developed and organized into a mesoscale convective system (MCS) west of the area, then moved through areas southwest of a line from Lexington, Nebraska to Beloit, Kansas after 10pm CDT. This activity was strong, but remained non-severe, over south central Nebraska.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe wind gusts occurred with a line of thunderstorms across portions of south central Nebraska and north central Kansas on the evening of July 2nd. Fairly typical summertime weather pattern occurred on this day in which scattered thunderstorms developed over the High Plains during the late afternoon, posing a tornado and large hail threat, then organized into a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) as they shifted eastward through the evening into an increasing moist, and unstable environment. As is often the case with QLCS events in this part of the country during this time of year, severe wind gusts were the primary threat. Thunderstorm mergers over extreme southwest Nebraska and northwest Kansas promoted the development of a significant pool of cold air which caused a segment of thunderstorms, within the broader line of thunderstorms, to quickly accelerate eastward into western portions of the County Warning Area (CWA) between 7-8pm CDT. By the time this activity arrived it already had a history of producing widespread 60-70mph wind gusts, and even isolated 70-80+mph wind gusts in the vicinity of Hill City, Kansas. The bowing line of thunderstorms continued to produce a swath of severe winds as it moved through the southern half (or so) of the CWA through the late evening hours. For south central Nebraska, severe reports were confined to areas mainly along and south of Highway 6 in Furnas and Harlan Counties, though a Cooperative Observer in Gosper County estimated up to 70mph wind gusts. Damage to trees and power lines were the main impacts of the severe wind. The strongest thunderstorm cores, on the leading edge of the QLCS, exited the area to the southeast by around midnight. Trailing stratiform rain lingered for a couple more hours.||As mentioned above, the meteorological conditions were quite typical for this area for early July. A warm and moist air mass and steep mid level lapse rates supported very strong instability (MLCAPE 4000-5000 J/kg) across a large portion of the Central Plains. A broad upper level ridge axis was in place from the southern Plains into the Upper Mississippi River Valley, resulting in weak southwesterly flow aloft. Because of the upper ridging, deep layer wind shear was weak at less than 30 knots, with much of the shear owing to veering of wind direction in the low to mid levels. This indicates that primarily mesoscale features such as an MCV and a strong cold pool were the main factors that supported severe wind in this event, and not synoptic scale features. Downdraft CAPE values in excess of 1500 J/kg were indicative of the strong potential for cool pool development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be near 70 MPH. A tree approximately 10 inches in diameter was snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with damaging winds affected areas along Interstate 80 during the early morning hours of July 10th. Thunderstorms initially developed over the western Sandhills in central Nebraska during the late afternoon and early evening hours of July 9th. Two supercells merged northwest of North Platte, which resulted in one, forward moving supercell that transitioned to produce intense winds. 80-90mph winds were noted in the North Platte area, including an 87mph gust measured at the North Platte Airport. This activity continued to produce intense damaging wind as it moved southeastward into western Dawson County around 1am CDT on July 10th. The most intense winds occurred in the Cozad area with measured gusts up to 75mph resulting in widespread, significant tree damage in addition to building, power pole, and crop damage. Also, a semi tractor trailer was overturned on Interstate 80 west of Cozad. Another overturned semi tractor trailer was reported just west of Lexington. No injuries or fatalities were reported. Damage in Lexington was not as intense and limited to mainly tree damage with a measured gust at the Lexington Airport of 59mph, respectively. Thunderstorms continued to weaken as they shifted east, with only sporadic, minor tree damage noted in Kearney around 2am CDT. Thunderstorms dissipated to weak showers by 3am CDT.||Overall, it appears that this thunderstorm activity overachieved it���s meteorological environment, at least thermodynamically. The main instability axis of moderate MLCAPE of 1000-2000 J/kg was located primarily across the western High Plains, with generally less than 1000 J/kg of MLCAPE noted across western portions of the Hastings CWA. Instability quickly decreased with eastern extent, which likely led to the rapid decrease in thunderstorm intensity between Lexington and Kearney. However, what was lacking in the thermodynamic environment was likely compensated for kinematically as deep layer shear was impressive at 50-60 kt owing to significant veering of winds from southeast near to surface to west-northwest in the mid levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be near 65 MPH caused an overturned semi trailer near Interstate 80 mile marker 218. This overturned trailer blocked Interstate 80 traffic.
