Within the broader zone of ice damage, a smaller swath around 30-40 miles wide of more severe impacts appears to have occurred from Adams and Clay Counties northeast to portions of York and Polk Counties. This is where large tree limbs (10 to 15 inches in diameter) were snapped and where power outages were most common and long-lasting, some in excess of 24 hours (mainly rural areas). Also, some of the large tree limbs fell onto roadways and further complicated already hazardous travel conditions. A Cooperative Observer just northeast of Shelby, NE (in Polk County) reported ice accumulation as high as one half inch. School and business closures were numerous not only in the areas affected by ice, but also further north where snow was more prevalent. Meteorologically speaking, this event was impressive due to very high amounts of moisture, and even some instability, that overlapped with near or below freezing temperatures at the surface. Precipitable water values (PWATs) were as high as 1.0 to 1.2 inches, again, anomalously high for November. While this event was certainly towards the higher end of recent ice events, the area was fortunate it wasn���t worse for several reasons: 1) Rain rates, while impressive, were actually too high for optimal and efficient ice accretion 2) surface temperatures were generally one to two degrees too warm for optimal ice accretion 3) Surface winds were relatively light and 4) duration of ice accretion was limited to around 6 hrs. We can say with relatively high confidence that if even one of the above factors was worse, this event could���ve been much more severe. Finally, it's worth noting that for many, this was the most significant precipitation event in around two months, as many areas along and east of Highway 281 received 0.75��� to 1��� or more of moisture.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain and freezing drizzle resulted in a coating of ice at least one-quarter inch thick. Travel was hazardous, and numerous tree limbs were downed. Power lines were also affected, causing power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and plentiful moisture combined to produce a mix of wintery weather and heavy rain across south central Nebraska on November 9th into the 10th. Strong southerly wind gusts from the previous day (see November 8th Storm Data entry) continued into the early morning hours of November 9th, which allowed for copious amounts of atmospheric moisture to advect into the region ahead of a cold front. The cold front moved through the area from northwest to southeast during the morning and afternoon hours, with little in the way of precipitation. The front slowed down by late afternoon as surface low pressure organized over the Texas and Oklahoma Panhandles, and the cold front eventually stalled out from central Kansas to central Iowa, just southeast of the NWS Hastings County Warning Area (CWA). This resulted in a sharp temperature gradient at the surface across the area, with cold air in the 20s across northwest portions of the CWA, 30s to low 40s across the far southeast CWA, and the surface freezing line from north central Kansas to east central Nebraska. Later during the evening hours, showers and even weak thunderstorms blossomed as moisture and weak instability interacted with the stalled cold front and shallow low level cold air mass. This set the stage for a wintry mix of sleet and freezing rain across south central Nebraska during the evening hours of November 9th. Rain rates were impressive by November standards, and with surface temperatures below freezing, ice accretion began quickly. The greatest ice accretion occurred from around 8pm on the 9th until 2am on the 10th before mid level dry air was advected in from the southwest. The system was not done causing wintry weather across the area, however, as the surface and upper level low pressure ejected northeast from the southern High Plains into the Lower MO Valley and wrap around moisture brought a period of light to moderate snow during the morning hours of the 10, mainly after 4am. Several inches of snow fell across areas mainly north of Interstate 80, with the highest amounts of 4 to 6 inches observed in Greeley County. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Non-thunderstorm high winds associated with a passing cold front occurred in southwestern Nebraska during the afternoon hours of November 14th, 2020. The maximum sustained wind speed recorded was 43 mph and the maximum wind gust speed recorded was 62 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were seen across Dundy county based high winds observed in the surrounding counties. Event time and wind speed estimate were based on observations from these surrounding counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season winter storm impacted the region on the 8th and 9th bringing in mixed precipitation and accumulating snowfall. In addition in the wake of the precipitation, hard freezing temperatures brought an end to the growing season with many locations dropping overnight temperatures into the middle 20s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer located in Harrison measured a total storm snowfall of 7.7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During November, 18 of the 24 counties in South Central Nebraska were completely-or-partially included in D2-or-worse drought, per weekly issuances of the U.S. Drought Monitor. For much of the western half of the area (especially west of a Greeley-Minden-Franklin line), November marked at least the fourth-consecutive month of below normal precipitation, prompting the return of D3 Extreme Drought to portions of South Central Nebraska for the first time since 2013. Meanwhile, for roughly the eastern half of the area, November precipitation trends were more encouraging as most places registered slightly wetter-than-normal for the month (totals commonly 1.50-2.00 inches). Of note, November finally broke a five-month streak of below normal precipitation at Hastings that had lasted from June-October. Although drought categories did not explicitly improve in the eastern half of the area, they at least remained status quo, stemming a gradual-worsening trend that had defined the preceding few months. ||Breaking things down in more detail and focusing on specific drought categories: Clearly the most newsworthy November note was the late-month introduction of D3 to all of of Furnas/Gosper counties, along with adjacent portions of Dawson/Phelps/Harlan/far southwestern Buffalo (total of six affected counties). Within much of this area, November precipitation totals were incredibly meager, including as little as 0.04 at Wilsonville and 0.07 south of Elwood. However, the introduction of D3 was ultimately driven by slightly-longer term precipitation deficits, as several official NWS stations in the D3 area endured one of their driest August-November stretches on record, including record-driest at Elwood 8S (1.47) and Canaday Steam Plant/Lexington 6SSE (1.07). Slightly east of the official D3 area, Minden also notched its driest-on-record Aug.-Nov. with only 2.30. In addition to the six counties included in D3 by month's end, all-or-some of 12 other counties west of the Highway 81 corridor remained in D2 Severe Drought throughout the month. Meanwhile, the rest of South Central Nebraska remained in (or was newly-assigned) D1 Moderate Drought during November. Temperature-wise, although not as big of a drought-factor as it would have been during the growing season, November was certainly a warm one with most local stations averaging 5-7 degrees F above normal (among the Top-10 warmest-on-record at many of them).

A cold front brought strong northwesterly wind gusts during the late afternoon on November 14th. A strong low pressure system across southern Canada forced a cold front through south central Nebraska during the late afternoon hours. Strong northwest wind gusts accompanied the passage of the front, particularly across the western third of the County Warning Area (CWA), where a few gusts of 55 to 65 mph were measured. There was no known damage or impacts from the high wind. The severe wind gusts were supported by intense pressure rises on the order of 4-5mb per 2 hours. The arrival time of the front during or just after peak mixing was also a contributing factor. Peak wind gusts further east were weaker, but still strong, at speeds of 50 to 55 mph, as far east as Highway 281. Peak wind gusts remained less than 50mph east of Highway 281 as the 2hr pressure rises decreased to 2-3 mb.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 63 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located 1 mile north-northeast of Cozad.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms formed over the Colorado High Plains increasing in coverage to form a line and intensity as they moved east into Kansas and Nebraska. In Kansas, the storms produced up to quarter sized hail, up to 74 mph wind gusts, and up to 1.1 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail from penny to quarter sized fell for a minimum duration of 3 minutes at the location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front moving eastward through southwestern Nebraska. These thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts with speeds up to 74 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KMCK ASOS reported a 63 mph wind gust at location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front moving eastward through southwestern Nebraska. These thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts with speeds up to 74 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree limb down that was roughly 3 car lengths in length. Speed estimated based on damage indicator.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation continued well-below normal across northeast Nebraska, varying from 25 to 50 percent of normal. Temperatures were mainly near to slightly below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought (D2) started the month with continued very warm and dry conditions. Drought conditions were alleviated somewhat by mid-month by rainfall which brought 1.90 inches at Emerson and 1.60 inches at Sioux City between September 8-12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initiated along a dry line in central Colorado moving east and forming a cluster of supercells that moved through Dundy, Hitchcock, and Red Willow counties along the Kansas and Nebraska border. The storms produced up to tennis ball sized hail and wind gusts up to 91 mph that blew out windows and garage doors, caused power poles to lean, power outages, and blew down trees along with various sizes of branches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Citizen observed an estimated wind gust of 60 mph in the core of a thundestorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed along the dry line in central Colorado increasing in coverage as they moved east. The cells merged into clusters along a line as they entered southwest Nebraska and northwest Kansas. The storms produced up to 79 mph wind gusts in Dundy, Hitchcock, and Red Willow counties. The 79 mph gust in Red Willow County caused snapped a power pole and downed various sized of tree limbs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported tree limbs of various sizes down along with a snapped power pole in northern McCook. The damage occurred near the time of a measured 79 mph wind gust at the McCook ASOS station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elevated thunderstorm developed on the combined boundary of a cold front and outflow from earlier night convection. A strong updraft congealed and resulted in spotty large hail from northeast Nebraska into northwest Iowa.

With continued dry conditions in September, severe and extreme drought conditions expanded across the eastern Nebraska Panhandle and portions of the western Sandhills and southwestern Nebraska in September. Extreme drought conditions expanded into the western third of Garden and extreme southwestern portions of Sheridan and extreme northwestern portions of Deuel county by the end of September. East of this area to a line from Gordon, to Arthur, to North Platte and Maywood, severe drought conditions had developed by the end of month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the month of September, severe drought (D2) conditions, expanded into all of Perkins County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initiated along a dry line in central Colorado moving east and forming a cluster of supercells that moved through Dundy, Hitchcock, and Red Willow counties along the Kansas and Nebraska border. The storms produced up to tennis ball sized hail and wind gusts up to 91 mph that blew out windows and garage doors, caused power poles to lean, power outages, and blew down trees along with various sizes of branches.

Although most of South Central Nebraska experienced a dry August-September, September marked the fourth consecutive month of below normal rainfall within a relatively-focused zone through the heart of the 24-county area. As a result, the U.S. Drought Monitor gradually expanded the coverage of D2 Severe Drought over the course of the month. As September opened, D2 drought was already assigned to much of Kearney/Adams counties, and adjacent smaller portions of Buffalo, Hall and Clay counties. On the 8th, D2 expanded slightly to also include small portions of Phelps, Franklin and Webster counties. Finally, one more small expansion of D2 on the 22nd brought northeastern Harlan County into the mix as well, making for a total of nine counties at least partially included in Severe Drought for at least some portion of the month. Outside of the worst-off D2 area, both D1 Moderate Drought and D0 Abnormally Dry also expanded across much of South Central Nebraska during September. Of all 24 local counties, only the majority of Thayer County managed to remain void of all drought categories (including D0) through the month. ||Digging deeper into September rainfall, although the month was not as acutely dry as August, the majority of the area was again below-average, with most places tallying between 30-80 percent of normal. In general, the overall-driest official NWS totals focused within northern/western counties, including 0.57 at Ord airport, 0.66 eight miles south of Elwood and 0.67 at Miller. On the opposite end of the spectrum, parts of Thayer County featured the only legitimate above normal amounts in South Central Nebraska, including as much as 3.45 at Hebron. Over the longer-term four-month period from June-September, some of the driest portions of the area only received 4-6 of rain (generally 35-45 percent of normal). This included only 4.92 at Minden (2nd-driest on record), 4.97 at Kearney airport (5th-driest), 5.58 in St. Paul (4th-driest) and 5.66 at Hastings airport (outright-driest). Fortunately, hot temperatures were not a major factor contributing to drought stress for the majority of September, as most of the area observed monthly departures from near-normal to 2 degrees cooler-than-normal. However, a notable exception was the week of the 20th-26th, during which every day saw highs into at least the 80s (and even a few 90s). Although there were no widespread/major drought impacts noted within South Central Nebraska during September, pastures and dryland crops exhibited continued stress as the growing season drew to a close, and some area rivers/streams reported below normal stream flows (including the Little Blue River).

As September opened, D2 drought was already assigned to much of Kearney/Adams counties, and adjacent smaller portions of Buffalo, Hall and Clay counties. On the 8th, D2 expanded slightly to also include small portions of Phelps, Franklin and Webster counties. Finally, one more small expansion of D2 on the 22nd brought northeastern Harlan County into the mix as well, making for a total of nine counties at least partially included in Severe Drought for at least some portion of the month. Outside of the worst-off D2 area, both D1 Moderate Drought and D0 Abnormally Dry also expanded across much of South Central Nebraska during September. Of all 24 local counties, only the majority of Thayer County managed to remain void of all drought categories (including D0) through the month. ||Digging deeper into September rainfall, although the month was not as acutely dry as August, the majority of the area was again below-average, with most places tallying between 30-80 percent of normal. In general, the overall-driest official NWS totals focused within northern/western counties, including 0.57 at Ord airport, 0.66 eight miles south of Elwood and 0.67 at Miller. On the opposite end of the spectrum, parts of Thayer County featured the only legitimate above normal amounts in South Central Nebraska, including as much as 3.45 at Hebron. Over the longer-term four-month period from June-September, some of the driest portions of the area only received 4-6 of rain (generally 35-45 percent of normal). This included only 4.92 at Minden (2nd-driest on record), 4.97 at Kearney airport (5th-driest), 5.58 in St. Paul (4th-driest) and 5.66 at Hastings airport (outright-driest). Fortunately, hot temperatures were not a major factor contributing to drought stress for the majority of September, as most of the area observed monthly departures from near-normal to 2 degrees cooler-than-normal. However, a notable exception was the week of the 20th-26th, during which every day saw highs into at least the 80s (and even a few 90s). Although there were no widespread/major drought impacts noted within South Central Nebraska during September, pastures and dryland crops exhibited continued stress as the growing season drew to a close, and some area rivers/streams reported below normal stream flows (including the Little Blue River).

Thunderstorms initiated along a dry line in central Colorado moving east and forming a cluster of supercells that moved through Dundy, Hitchcock, and Red Willow counties along the Kansas and Nebraska border. The storms produced up to tennis ball sized hail and wind gusts up to 91 mph that blew out windows and garage doors, caused power poles to lean, power outages, and blew down trees along with various sizes of branches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Citizen reported quarter sized hail that stripped corn crop of all its leaves.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Citizen reported hail ranging from dime to quarter in size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed along the dry line in central Colorado increasing in coverage as they moved east. The cells merged into clusters along a line as they entered southwest Nebraska and northwest Kansas. The storms produced up to 79 mph wind gusts in Dundy, Hitchcock, and Red Willow counties. The 79 mph gust in Red Willow County caused snapped a power pole and downed various sized of tree limbs.

Lift associated with a jet streak across southern North Dakota interacted with an unstable and weakly capped environment with 30 to 40 knots of deep-layer shear to produce a couple of smaller clusters of thunderstorms with large hail and damaging wind during the evening and overnight which progressed east and southeast across southeast South Dakota into northeast Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail accompanied by estimated wind gusts up to 50 mph. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in southwestern Nebraska and traveled south-southeast toward the NE/KS border. Thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts estimated at 70 mph that caused damage to trees in Bartley, NE.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported numerous tree limbs down due to straight line winds at location. Spotter also reported pea to dime size hail. Speed estimated by storm damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening a line of thunderstorms moved south into Southwest Nebraska.  As the storms moved through they produced hail up to ping-pong ball size, estimated wind gusts up to 60 MPH, and started an oil supply tank on fire near Trenton from a lightning strike.  Part of this line of storms moved slow enough to cause flash flooding near Parks in Rock Creek, washing part of the road and small bridge out.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell for a duration of 5 minutes at the location. The hail and severe wind damaged two windows at the residence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening a line of thunderstorms moved south into Southwest Nebraska. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail ranged from half inch to one inch in size.  Wind gusts of 40-50 MPH also occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds affected portions of south central Nebraska during the late morning and early afternoon hours on Sunday, November 8th. Peak wind gusts of 60 to 65 mph were observed by airport ASOS/AWOS stations as well as various mesonet stations between roughly 9am and 2pm CST. The primary areas affected were east of Highway 183. No significant damage was reported as a result of these strong winds.||The strong southerly winds occurred within a broad warm sector of an intense low pressure system tracking from the northern Rockies into the northern Plains. An anomalously strong low level jet developed over the Central Plains during the night of November 7th into the early morning hours of November 8th, in response to an increasing pressure gradient, and peaked at a magnitude of 60 to 70kt. Boundary layer mixing depth increased after sunrise on the morning of November 8th and managed to tap into the bottom of this low level jet, with the result being several hours of severe southerly synoptic winds being mixed down to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be near 60 MPH, based on neighboring observations, occurred during the late morning through early afternoon hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms became stationary over Dundy County during the evening of the 23rd.  The storm activity spread to the south and west through the rest of the 23rd into the 24th before ending from west to east.  When the rainfall ended, over nine inches of rain had occurred roughly along CR 709, with lesser amounts to the north and south.  Roads were washed out in many places, and the AM Track train was stranded at Haigler due to the tracks being washed out somewhere between Haigler and Parks.  Highway 34 was closed from Benkelman to Highway 385 in Yuma County Colorado due to flooding. The Republican River at Benkelman reached a peak height of almost nine and a half feet. |The flood waters extended east into Hitchcock County, where the river gauge at Stratton measured a stage just below the record stage of 11 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from prolonged heavy rainfall in Dundy County caused the Republican River to surpass flood stage in the western part of Hitchcock County, from the county line to Stratton. The flood waters did not cause any damage to buildings, roads, or bridges along the river channel. In addition, the river stage at Stratton was just under 11 feet, which is just below the record stage for the site.

No significant damage was reported as a result of these strong winds.||The strong southerly winds occurred within a broad warm sector of an intense low pressure system tracking from the northern Rockies into the northern Plains. An anomalously strong low level jet developed over the Central Plains during the night of November 7th into the early morning hours of November 8th, in response to an increasing pressure gradient, and peaked at a magnitude of 60 to 70kt. Boundary layer mixing depth increased after sunrise on the morning of November 8th and managed to tap into the bottom of this low level jet, with the result being several hours of severe southerly synoptic winds being mixed down to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be near 60 MPH occurred during the late morning through early afternoon hours. A wind gust of 59 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located 2 miles southwest of Grand Island at 11:04 AM CST, and a wind gust of 58 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located in Doniphan at 11:03 AM CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms became stationary over Dundy County during the evening of the 23rd.  The storm activity spread to the south and west through the rest of the 23rd into the 24th before ending from west to east.  When the rainfall ended, over nine inches of rain had occurred roughly along CR 709, with lesser amounts to the north and south.  Roads were washed out in many places, and the AM Track train was stranded at Haigler due to the tracks being washed out somewhere between Haigler and Parks.  Highway 34 was closed from Benkelman to Highway 385 in Yuma County Colorado due to flooding. The Republican River at Benkelman reached a peak height of almost nine and a half feet. |The flood waters extended east into Hitchcock County, where the river gauge at Stratton measured a stage just below the record stage of 11 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from prolonged heavy rainfall in Dundy County caused the Republican River to surpass flood stage in the western part of Hitchcock County, from the county line to Stratton.  The flood waters did not cause any damage to buildings, roads, or bridges along the river channel.  In addition, the river stage at Stratton was just under 11 feet, which is just below the record stage for the site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening a line of thunderstorms moved south into Southwest Nebraska.  As the storms moved through they produced hail up to ping-pong ball size, estimated wind gusts up to 60 MPH, and started an oil supply tank on fire near Trenton from a lightning strike.  Part of this line of storms moved slow enough to cause flash flooding near Parks in Rock Creek, washing part of the road and small bridge out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 50-60 MPH occurred with hail up to quarter size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initiated along a dry line in central Colorado moving east and forming a cluster of supercells that moved through Dundy, Hitchcock, and Red Willow counties along the Kansas and Nebraska border. The storms produced up to tennis ball sized hail and wind gusts up to 91 mph that blew out windows and garage doors, caused power poles to lean, power outages, and blew down trees along with various sizes of branches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported a 5 inch diameter tree limb blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe weather produced numerous wind and hail reports across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Utility company reported trees and large limbs down on power lines causing power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms developed and produced large hail and strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter estimated wind at about 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area bringing with it light snow fall and winds gusting up to 60 mph. Blizzard conditions were observed much of the area. Many other locations not in the blizzard warning, experience near blizzard conditions and had impacts warranting a Winter Storm warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Personal weather station measured peak wind of 50 mph. Light snow combined with strong winds led to a reduction of visibility and winter weather impacts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although it only impacted a small percentage of the 24-county South Central Nebraska area, the large and intense supercell storm that marched south-southwestward for over 90 miles through several eastern counties on this Friday afternoon/early evening was likely the local area's most noteworthy individual storm of the 2020 convective season. In addition to dropping three fairly brief EF-0 tornadoes in southeastern Hamilton and northeastern Clay counties, its main calling card was large hail, with several reports of golf ball to slightly larger than baseball size stones in Clay/Nuckolls counties, and smaller hail to around quarter size as far north as Osceola. Interestingly, damaging winds were not a major player, with no ground-truth gusts in excess of 50 MPH. Focusing on specific storm reports, and starting with the three EF-0 tornadoes, they all occurred between 5-6 p.m. CDT, touching down briefly along a roughly 15-mile long corridor from near Interstate 80 a few miles south of Hampton, down to the Saronville area. Per storm surveys, the first two tornadoes were each on the ground for roughly 2 miles. While the majority of minor damage was confined to crops and trees, a lean-to shed was destroyed and a pivot overturned. As for the hail, the largest reported was 3 diameter between Clay Center and Harvard, but towns including Sutton and Nelson endured stones to at least tennis ball size. Flooding was not much of an issue, as the storm was progressive enough to keep rainfall amounts largely under one inch. ||Breaking down the meteorological background and event timing, this was actually a fairly conditional severe weather setup for South Central Nebraska, with various pros and cons for/against robust activity. In the mid-upper levels, the local area resided a fair distance south of a vigorous shortwave trough swinging across the Dakotas towards northern MN, resulting in fairly limited forcing aloft. Also working against convection (especially widespread severe) was a plume of warm mid level temperatures, with 700 millibar values of 12-14C providing a healthy cap. On the other hand, low-level forcing was seasonably-strong, as a well-defined cold front slid southeastward into the area during the afternoon. Ahead of this boundary, a significantly-unstable airmass awaited, featuring temperatures in the upper 80s-low 90s F/dewpoints upper 60s-mid 70s F, yielding formidable mixed-layer CAPE around 4000 J/kg. This extreme instability, in the presence of sufficient deep-layer wind shear around 35 knots, clearly fostered a mesoscale environment capable of stout convection. As for what actually transpired, the main show was preceded by a broken line of non-severe, likely elevated storms that passed primarily through counties south of Interstate 80 and east of Highway 281 between 12:30-3:30 p.m. CDT. About the time this innocuous early afternoon activity was departing the local area to the southeast, a broken line of true, surface-based storms got going along the front in northeast Nebraska, with the southwestern-most cell building down into the far northeast corner of South Central Nebraska (eastern Nance/northeast Merrick/northern Polk counties) between 3-4 p.m. CDT. Within an hour thereafter, this tail end Charlie storm rapidly grew into a full-blown supercell over Polk/northern York counties, producing its first marginally-severe hail reports. During the following two hours (5-7 p.m. CDT), the storm anchored itself firmly to the surface front, with new updraft generation along its western edge promoting more of a southwestward propagation and causing it to grow larger in both intensity/size, becoming more of a supercell cluster. It was during this time that the brief tornadoes occurred along the storm's far western flank, likely driven by pronounced low-level vorticity stretching along the surface front. While the sporadic tornado touchdowns were brief, the storm continued to be a prolific hail-producer as it gradually exited the local area out of Nuckolls County into northern Kansas between 7-8 p.m. CDT, ending all South Central Nebraska convection for the night.

While the sporadic tornado touchdowns were brief, the storm continued to be a prolific hail-producer as it gradually exited the local area out of Nuckolls County into northern Kansas between 7-8 p.m. CDT, ending all South Central Nebraska convection for the night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging in size from quarters to tennis balls was reported in and around Nelson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening, and through following morning, multiple bands of moderate to heavy snowfall rates moved north over parts of Southwest Nebraska. Up to six inches of snow was reported from these bands in Dundy County, with a small portion extending into western Hitchcock County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across the county ranged from a couple inches in the northwest corner to six inches in the Benkelman area.  Key words:  heavy snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area bringing with it light snow fall and winds gusting up to 60 mph. Blizzard conditions were observed much of the area. Many other locations not in the blizzard warning, experience near blizzard conditions and had impacts warranting a Winter Storm warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS measured a peak wind gust of 48 mph and brief periods of visibilities near 0.25 miles. Light snow combined with strong winds led to a reduction of visibility and winter weather impacts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On this late Sunday afternoon and evening, several scattered, strong to severe storms advanced southward across various portions of South Central Nebraska, prompting many accounts of large hail and a few damaging wind gusts. While most hail was in the quarter to golf ball size range, larger stones of 2-3 diameter fell in the Chapman and Marquette areas of Merrick/Hamilton counties. Ping pong to golf ball size hail occurred in or near the following communities/locations: Fullerton, Amherst, Orleans, Harlan County Lake, Naponee and Deweese. In the Tri Cities, only Grand Island saw severe weather, as two rounds of quarter to half dollar size hail moved through, one mainly affecting the north side of town (including the airport) around 6 p.m. CDT, and another about an hour later that clipped more of the city. As for thunderstorm winds, the vast majority of gusts captured by area sensors were non-severe (generally 40-50 MPH), but there were two reports estimated around 60 MPH, one near Hampton and another at Hebron, where a few large trees were downed and a store front sustained minor damage. Given fairly progressive storm motions, rainfall totals in most affected areas were less than 1 inch during the afternoon/evening. However, combined with localized heavy rain that fell during a separate round of early morning/non-severe activity, portions of mainly eastern Clay County registered over 3 for the day (including NWS coop observer total of 3.33 six miles east-southeast of Clay Center). As a result, the later-day storms prompted some isolated flash flooding, as School Creek overtopped the paved spur road south of Saronville. ||This event was characterized by truly scattered, hit-or-miss convection, as afternoon heating and subtle convergence along/ahead of a weak cold front sagging southward into the region prompted several strong to severe storms, mainly in the form of multicells/multicell clusters, but also a few brief supercells. Nearly every county in the 24-county area observed at least a touch of thunderstorm activity. That being said, the majority of ground-truth reports concentrated in two primary areas: 1) parts of Hall/Hamilton/Merrick counties...2) a swath from eastern Furnas to far southwestern Franklin counties. A few of the first severe storms of the event flared up over Furnas/Buffalo counties between 430-530 p.m. CDT, and the final severe storm of the evening was centered over the Nuckolls County area before weakening around 9 p.m. CDT. Thereafter, a smattering of strong storms lingered within counties south of Interstate 80 through around 10:30 pm. CDT before all convection ended for the night. In total, 19 Severe Thunderstorm Warnings were issued within South Central Nebraska over the course of over four hours. Digging deeper into the meteorological setup, it's worth noting that this event exceeded forecast expectations, especially in terms of the number of severe storms. This was due in part to greater-than-expected convective instability, as afternoon surface dewpoints soared into the upper 60s-low 70 F,  promoting significant mixed-layer CAPE of at least 3000-4000 J/kg. In tandem with deep layer wind shear around 35 knots, this CAPE/shear combo proved plenty sufficient for robust convection. In the mid-upper levels, a pronounced north-northwest flow regime resided over the Central Plains, within the interface between a stout western United States ridge and a broad eastern trough.

A powerful storm system caused high winds across north central and southwest Nebraska. Northwest winds were frequently sustained at 30 to 40 mph with gusts to around 60 mph at times. The high winds began during the evening hours on December 22nd, and continued during the overnight hours into the afternoon hours on December 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph with gusts up to 71 mph occurred across Lincoln County during the morning into the early afternoon hours. A mesonet site 4 miles north of North Platte recorded a peak wind gust of 71 mph at 1115 am CST. The North Platte ASOS also recorded a peak wind gust of 67 mph at 1153 am CST.

Thereafter, a smattering of strong storms lingered within counties south of Interstate 80 through around 10:30 pm. CDT before all convection ended for the night. In total, 19 Severe Thunderstorm Warnings were issued within South Central Nebraska over the course of over four hours. Digging deeper into the meteorological setup, it's worth noting that this event exceeded forecast expectations, especially in terms of the number of severe storms. This was due in part to greater-than-expected convective instability, as afternoon surface dewpoints soared into the upper 60s-low 70 F,  promoting significant mixed-layer CAPE of at least 3000-4000 J/kg. In tandem with deep layer wind shear around 35 knots, this CAPE/shear combo proved plenty sufficient for robust convection. In the mid-upper levels, a pronounced north-northwest flow regime resided over the Central Plains, within the interface between a stout western United States ridge and a broad eastern trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be near 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On this late Sunday afternoon and evening, several scattered, strong to severe storms advanced southward across various portions of South Central Nebraska, prompting many accounts of large hail and a few damaging wind gusts. While most hail was in the quarter to golf ball size range, larger stones of 2-3 diameter fell in the Chapman and Marquette areas of Merrick/Hamilton counties. Ping pong to golf ball size hail occurred in or near the following communities/locations: Fullerton, Amherst, Orleans, Harlan County Lake, Naponee and Deweese. In the Tri Cities, only Grand Island saw severe weather, as two rounds of quarter to half dollar size hail moved through, one mainly affecting the north side of town (including the airport) around 6 p.m. CDT, and another about an hour later that clipped more of the city. As for thunderstorm winds, the vast majority of gusts captured by area sensors were non-severe (generally 40-50 MPH), but there were two reports estimated around 60 MPH, one near Hampton and another at Hebron, where a few large trees were downed and a store front sustained minor damage. Given fairly progressive storm motions, rainfall totals in most affected areas were less than 1 inch during the afternoon/evening. However, combined with localized heavy rain that fell during a separate round of early morning/non-severe activity, portions of mainly eastern Clay County registered over 3 for the day (including NWS coop observer total of 3.33 six miles east-southeast of Clay Center). As a result, the later-day storms prompted some isolated flash flooding, as School Creek overtopped the paved spur road south of Saronville. ||This event was characterized by truly scattered, hit-or-miss convection, as afternoon heating and subtle convergence along/ahead of a weak cold front sagging southward into the region prompted several strong to severe storms, mainly in the form of multicells/multicell clusters, but also a few brief supercells. Mainly between midnight and 4 a.m. CDT on this early Thursday morning, scattered strong-to-briefly-severe thunderstorms rumbled along a narrow corridor of South Central Nebraska from west-northwest to east-southeast, primarily affecting these counties: Dawson, Buffalo, Hall, Adams, Clay, Fillmore and Thayer. Although radar data suggested that spotty hail to at least nickel size likely occurred within various counties, ground-truth verification was quite limited, consisting of only a few reports of penny to quarter size stones in Hastings around 1:30 a.m. CDT, followed a short time later by the only severe-criteria report of the event: golf ball size hail at Inland in western Clay County. A few hours later (around 4 a.m. CDT), the final strong local storms of the morning dissipated over eastern Fillmore/Thayer counties. There were no reports of strong/severe wind gusts. ||This somewhat-unexpected nocturnal convection (at least in terms of intensity) initiated in the local area over Dawson County shortly before midnight CDT, roughly 50 miles east of a small cluster of strong storms already ongoing off to the west, and roughly 75-100 miles north of the centroid of a small mesoscale convective system (MCS) sliding across northern Kansas (in fact the local storms appeared to be a northward-displaced offshoot of the Kansas MCS). Whether or not the local convection was directly tied to the activity farther south, mesoscale analysis revealed that it was likely sparked by a subtle ribbon of low-mid level warm air/moisture advection within a weak southwesterly low level jet axis (winds at 850 millibars less than 25 knots). Convective parameters were at least marginally-supportive of sporadic intense updrafts, featuring around 1500 J/kg most-unstable CAPE and up to around 30 knots of deep-layer wind shear.

Although it only impacted a small percentage of the 24-county South Central Nebraska area, the large and intense supercell storm that marched south-southwestward for over 90 miles through several eastern counties on this Friday afternoon/early evening was likely the local area's most noteworthy individual storm of the 2020 convective season. In addition to dropping three fairly brief EF-0 tornadoes in southeastern Hamilton and northeastern Clay counties, its main calling card was large hail, with several reports of golf ball to slightly larger than baseball size stones in Clay/Nuckolls counties, and smaller hail to around quarter size as far north as Osceola. Interestingly, damaging winds were not a major player, with no ground-truth gusts in excess of 50 MPH. Focusing on specific storm reports, and starting with the three EF-0 tornadoes, they all occurred between 5-6 p.m. CDT, touching down briefly along a roughly 15-mile long corridor from near Interstate 80 a few miles south of Hampton, down to the Saronville area. Per storm surveys, the first two tornadoes were each on the ground for roughly 2 miles. While the majority of minor damage was confined to crops and trees, a lean-to shed was destroyed and a pivot overturned. As for the hail, the largest reported was 3 diameter between Clay Center and Harvard, but towns including Sutton and Nelson endured stones to at least tennis ball size. Flooding was not much of an issue, as the storm was progressive enough to keep rainfall amounts largely under one inch. ||Breaking down the meteorological background and event timing, this was actually a fairly conditional severe weather setup for South Central Nebraska, with various pros and cons for/against robust activity. In the mid-upper levels, the local area resided a fair distance south of a vigorous shortwave trough swinging across the Dakotas towards northern MN, resulting in fairly limited forcing aloft. Also working against convection (especially widespread severe) was a plume of warm mid level temperatures, with 700 millibar values of 12-14C providing a healthy cap. On the other hand, low-level forcing was seasonably-strong, as a well-defined cold front slid southeastward into the area during the afternoon. Ahead of this boundary, a significantly-unstable airmass awaited, featuring temperatures in the upper 80s-low 90s F/dewpoints upper 60s-mid 70s F, yielding formidable mixed-layer CAPE around 4000 J/kg. This extreme instability, in the presence of sufficient deep-layer wind shear around 35 knots, clearly fostered a mesoscale environment capable of stout convection. As for what actually transpired, the main show was preceded by a broken line of non-severe, likely elevated storms that passed primarily through counties south of Interstate 80 and east of Highway 281 between 12:30-3:30 p.m. CDT. About the time this innocuous early afternoon activity was departing the local area to the southeast, a broken line of true, surface-based storms got going along the front in northeast Nebraska, with the southwestern-most cell building down into the far northeast corner of South Central Nebraska (eastern Nance/northeast Merrick/northern Polk counties) between 3-4 p.m. CDT. Within an hour thereafter, this tail end Charlie storm rapidly grew into a full-blown supercell over Polk/northern York counties, producing its first marginally-severe hail reports. During the following two hours (5-7 p.m. CDT), the storm anchored itself firmly to the surface front, with new updraft generation along its western edge promoting more of a southwestward propagation and causing it to grow larger in both intensity/size, becoming more of a supercell cluster. It was during this time that the brief tornadoes occurred along the storm's far western flank, likely driven by pronounced low-level vorticity stretching along the surface front. While the sporadic tornado touchdowns were brief, the storm continued to be a prolific hail-producer as it gradually exited the local area out of Nuckolls County into northern Kansas between 7-8 p.m. CDT, ending all South Central Nebraska convection for the night.

A powerful storm system caused high winds across north central and southwest Nebraska. Northwest winds were frequently sustained at 30 to 40 mph with gusts to around 60 mph at times. The high winds began during the evening hours on December 22nd, and continued during the overnight hours into the afternoon hours on December 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds of 30 to 50 mph with gusts up to 71 mph occurred across Logan County during the morning into the early afternoon hours. A nearby mesonet site 4 miles north of North Platte recorded a peak wind gust of 71 mph at 1115 am CST. The nearby North Platte ASOS also recorded a peak wind gust of 67 mph at 11153 am MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions remained unchanged across western and north central Nebraska in December. Precipitation was below normal in the eastern Nebraska Panhandle and portions of far southwestern Nebraska. Across the northern Sandhills from eastern Cherry county into Holt county, precipitation for the month was running 50 to 90 percent of normal. Across the central and southern Sandhills, including Lincoln and Keith counties, precipitation was 100 to 150 percent of normal. For the year ending in December, Broken Bow, North Platte, Valentine and Imperial, ran precipitation deficits of: -3.33, -5.48, -1.94, and -9.36 inches respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the month of December, extreme (D3) drought conditions remained present over far southern Keith County. Over the remainder of the county, severe (D2) drought conditions persisted through December. The cooperative observer at Kingsley Dam reported 0.58 inches of precipitation in December. This was 0.12 inches above the normal of 0.46 inches. The cooperative station at Ogallala reported 0.69 inches and was 0.30 inches above the normal of 0.39 inches.

This was due in part to greater-than-expected convective instability, as afternoon surface dewpoints soared into the upper 60s-low 70 F,  promoting significant mixed-layer CAPE of at least 3000-4000 J/kg. In tandem with deep layer wind shear around 35 knots, this CAPE/shear combo proved plenty sufficient for robust convection. In the mid-upper levels, a pronounced north-northwest flow regime resided over the Central Plains, within the interface between a stout western United States ridge and a broad eastern trough.

On this late Sunday afternoon and evening, several scattered, strong to severe storms advanced southward across various portions of South Central Nebraska, prompting many accounts of large hail and a few damaging wind gusts. While most hail was in the quarter to golf ball size range, larger stones of 2-3 diameter fell in the Chapman and Marquette areas of Merrick/Hamilton counties. Ping pong to golf ball size hail occurred in or near the following communities/locations: Fullerton, Amherst, Orleans, Harlan County Lake, Naponee and Deweese. In the Tri Cities, only Grand Island saw severe weather, as two rounds of quarter to half dollar size hail moved through, one mainly affecting the north side of town (including the airport) around 6 p.m. CDT, and another about an hour later that clipped more of the city. As for thunderstorm winds, the vast majority of gusts captured by area sensors were non-severe (generally 40-50 MPH), but there were two reports estimated around 60 MPH, one near Hampton and another at Hebron, where a few large trees were downed and a store front sustained minor damage. Given fairly progressive storm motions, rainfall totals in most affected areas were less than 1 inch during the afternoon/evening. However, combined with localized heavy rain that fell during a separate round of early morning/non-severe activity, portions of mainly eastern Clay County registered over 3 for the day (including NWS coop observer total of 3.33 six miles east-southeast of Clay Center). As a result, the later-day storms prompted some isolated flash flooding, as School Creek overtopped the paved spur road south of Saronville. ||This event was characterized by truly scattered, hit-or-miss convection, as afternoon heating and subtle convergence along/ahead of a weak cold front sagging southward into the region prompted several strong to severe storms, mainly in the form of multicells/multicell clusters, but also a few brief supercells. Nearly every county in the 24-county area observed at least a touch of thunderstorm activity. That being said, the majority of ground-truth reports concentrated in two primary areas: 1) parts of Hall/Hamilton/Merrick counties...2) a swath from eastern Furnas to far southwestern Franklin counties. A few of the first severe storms of the event flared up over Furnas/Buffalo counties between 430-530 p.m. CDT, and the final severe storm of the evening was centered over the Nuckolls County area before weakening around 9 p.m. CDT. Thereafter, a smattering of strong storms lingered within counties south of Interstate 80 through around 10:30 pm. CDT before all convection ended for the night. In the mid-upper levels, a pronounced north-northwest flow regime resided over the Central Plains, within the interface between a stout western United States ridge and a broad eastern trough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between the late evening of Sunday the 30th and the pre-dawn hours of Monday the 31st, a cluster of strong thunderstorms tracked across mainly the northeastern one-third of South Central Nebraska (primarily northeast of an Ord-York line), producing slightly-sub-severe hail, gusty winds and brief heavy rain. Early in the event (12-1230 a.m. CDT), a few hail reports flirted with severe criteria, including nickel size stones in North Loup and dime size in Ord. All known wind gusts from area sensors registered under 50 MPH. ||Breaking down event timing, it's worth noting that north-northwest local counties had already dodged a possible severe storm threat earlier in the evening, as a broken line of strong to severe storms approaching from central/north central Nebraska fizzled out shortly before reaching the Valley County area around nightfall. A few hours later and shortly before midnight CDT, a few briefly-isolated strong storms rapidly flared up over Valley/Greeley counties, with the western one dropping the aforementioned dime-nickel size hail. Already by 1230 a.m. CDT these cells had merged into a small cluster, and although radar signatures suggested possible severe hail and/or wind across southern Greeley/far northern Howard counties, there were no verifying reports. All convection departed South Central Nebraska out of Polk/York counties between 3-4 a.m. CDT. In the mid-upper levels, this overnight event was fairly strongly-forced, as the southern edge of a potent shortwave trough swept through the Dakotas and far northern Nebraska. At the surface, storms initiated near/along an associated strong surface cold front sweeping through South Central Nebraska, its nocturnal passage marked by 25-40 MPH northerly gusts. Around the time of the hail in Valley County, the mesoscale environment featured at least 1500 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and around 40 knots of deep-layer wind shear.

A couple of strong (one briefly severe) thunderstorms infiltrated the far western and northern fringes of South Central Nebraska for a short time on this Tuesday evening, with one of them yielding a lone marginally-severe wind report in Furnas County. This storm entered the county from the west around 9 p.m. CDT as part of a north-south oriented, broken line of convection that initiated hours earlier in northeast Colorado. As the storm tracked along Highway 6/34, a county deputy estimated 60 MPH gusts near Holbrook. Shortly thereafter the storm weakened, and had all but dissipated before reaching Phelps County around 10 p.m. CDT. About an hour later, a completely separate storm (part of a complex that had been drifting across northwestern Nebraska for several hours) entered Valley County from the west-northwest. Although radar briefly suggested it might have reached severe levels, the only known report consisted of pea size hail near Elyria. Like the other storm, this one also weakened considerably within an hour of entering the local area, and there was no additional strong convection  within South Central Nebraska the remainder of the night. In the mid-upper levels, this was a typical summer setup for scattered, loosely-organized convection to survive eastward into the local area off the High Plains during the evening, as weak/low-amplitude disturbances meandered through the prevailing quasi-zonal flow. Post-sunset mesoscale parameters over South Central Nebraska were only marginally-supportive of strong to severe storms, featuring around 1500 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE but no more than 25-30 knots of deep-layer wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Officer reported wind gusts of 60 to 70 MPH while driving between Cambridge and Arapahoe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds developed behind a cold front during the late morning hours into the afternoon hours on November 14th, 2020. Northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to around 60 mph occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The nearby Ogallala AWOS recorded a peak wind gust of 59 mph at 115 pm MST. Northwest winds of 30 to 40 mph sustained with gusts as high as 59 mph were common across Arthur County between 11 am until 3 pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds developed behind a cold front during the late morning hours into the afternoon hours on November 14th, 2020. Northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to around 60 mph occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The nearby Sidney ASOS recorded a peak wind gust of 68 mph at 1201 pm MST. Northwest winds of at least 40 mph sustained with gusts to around 60 mph were common across the southern half of Garden County between 11 am until 1230 pm MST. The strong winds also caused areas of blowing dust with visibilities less than a mile at times during the strongest winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although it only impacted a small percentage of the 24-county South Central Nebraska area, the large and intense supercell storm that marched south-southwestward for over 90 miles through several eastern counties on this Friday afternoon/early evening was likely the local area's most noteworthy individual storm of the 2020 convective season. In addition to dropping three fairly brief EF-0 tornadoes in southeastern Hamilton and northeastern Clay counties, its main calling card was large hail, with several reports of golf ball to slightly larger than baseball size stones in Clay/Nuckolls counties, and smaller hail to around quarter size as far north as Osceola. Interestingly, damaging winds were not a major player, with no ground-truth gusts in excess of 50 MPH. Focusing on specific storm reports, and starting with the three EF-0 tornadoes, they all occurred between 5-6 p.m. CDT, touching down briefly along a roughly 15-mile long corridor from near Interstate 80 a few miles south of Hampton, down to the Saronville area. Per storm surveys, the first two tornadoes were each on the ground for roughly 2 miles. While the majority of minor damage was confined to crops and trees, a lean-to shed was destroyed and a pivot overturned. As for the hail, the largest reported was 3 diameter between Clay Center and Harvard, but towns including Sutton and Nelson endured stones to at least tennis ball size. Flooding was not much of an issue, as the storm was progressive enough to keep rainfall amounts largely under one inch. ||Breaking down the meteorological background and event timing, this was actually a fairly conditional severe weather setup for South Central Nebraska, with various pros and cons for/against robust activity. In the mid-upper levels, the local area resided a fair distance south of a vigorous shortwave trough swinging across the Dakotas towards northern MN, resulting in fairly limited forcing aloft. Also working against convection (especially widespread severe) was a plume of warm mid level temperatures, with 700 millibar values of 12-14C providing a healthy cap. On the other hand, low-level forcing was seasonably-strong, as a well-defined cold front slid southeastward into the area during the afternoon. Ahead of this boundary, a significantly-unstable airmass awaited, featuring temperatures in the upper 80s-low 90s F/dewpoints upper 60s-mid 70s F, yielding formidable mixed-layer CAPE around 4000 J/kg. This extreme instability, in the presence of sufficient deep-layer wind shear around 35 knots, clearly fostered a mesoscale environment capable of stout convection. As for what actually transpired, the main show was preceded by a broken line of non-severe, likely elevated storms that passed primarily through counties south of Interstate 80 and east of Highway 281 between 12:30-3:30 p.m. CDT. About the time this innocuous early afternoon activity was departing the local area to the southeast, a broken line of true, surface-based storms got going along the front in northeast Nebraska, with the southwestern-most cell building down into the far northeast corner of South Central Nebraska (eastern Nance/northeast Merrick/northern Polk counties) between 3-4 p.m. CDT. Within an hour thereafter, this tail end Charlie storm rapidly grew into a full-blown supercell over Polk/northern York counties, producing its first marginally-severe hail reports. During the following two hours (5-7 p.m. CDT), the storm anchored itself firmly to the surface front, with new updraft generation along its western edge promoting more of a southwestward propagation and causing it to grow larger in both intensity/size, becoming more of a supercell cluster. It was during this time that the brief tornadoes occurred along the storm's far western flank, likely driven by pronounced low-level vorticity stretching along the surface front. While the sporadic tornado touchdowns were brief, the storm continued to be a prolific hail-producer as it gradually exited the local area out of Nuckolls County into northern Kansas between 7-8 p.m. CDT, ending all South Central Nebraska convection for the night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mainly between midnight and 4 a.m. CDT on this early Thursday morning, scattered strong-to-briefly-severe thunderstorms rumbled along a narrow corridor of South Central Nebraska from west-northwest to east-southeast, primarily affecting these counties: Dawson, Buffalo, Hall, Adams, Clay, Fillmore and Thayer. Although radar data suggested that spotty hail to at least nickel size likely occurred within various counties, ground-truth verification was quite limited, consisting of only a few reports of penny to quarter size stones in Hastings around 1:30 a.m. CDT, followed a short time later by the only severe-criteria report of the event: golf ball size hail at Inland in western Clay County. A few hours later (around 4 a.m. CDT), the final strong local storms of the morning dissipated over eastern Fillmore/Thayer counties. There were no reports of strong/severe wind gusts. ||This somewhat-unexpected nocturnal convection (at least in terms of intensity) initiated in the local area over Dawson County shortly before midnight CDT, roughly 50 miles east of a small cluster of strong storms already ongoing off to the west, and roughly 75-100 miles north of the centroid of a small mesoscale convective system (MCS) sliding across northern Kansas (in fact the local storms appeared to be a northward-displaced offshoot of the Kansas MCS). Whether or not the local convection was directly tied to the activity farther south, mesoscale analysis revealed that it was likely sparked by a subtle ribbon of low-mid level warm air/moisture advection within a weak southwesterly low level jet axis (winds at 850 millibars less than 25 knots). Convective parameters were at least marginally-supportive of sporadic intense updrafts, featuring around 1500 J/kg most-unstable CAPE and up to around 30 knots of deep-layer wind shear.

About the time this innocuous early afternoon activity was departing the local area to the southeast, a broken line of true, surface-based storms got going along the front in northeast Nebraska, with the southwestern-most cell building down into the far northeast corner of South Central Nebraska (eastern Nance/northeast Merrick/northern Polk counties) between 3-4 p.m. CDT. Within an hour thereafter, this tail end Charlie storm rapidly grew into a full-blown supercell over Polk/northern York counties, producing its first marginally-severe hail reports. During the following two hours (5-7 p.m. CDT), the storm anchored itself firmly to the surface front, with new updraft generation along its western edge promoting more of a southwestward propagation and causing it to grow larger in both intensity/size, becoming more of a supercell cluster. It was during this time that the brief tornadoes occurred along the storm's far western flank, likely driven by pronounced low-level vorticity stretching along the surface front. While the sporadic tornado touchdowns were brief, the storm continued to be a prolific hail-producer as it gradually exited the local area out of Nuckolls County into northern Kansas between 7-8 p.m. CDT, ending all South Central Nebraska convection for the night.

Although it only impacted a small percentage of the 24-county South Central Nebraska area, the large and intense supercell storm that marched south-southwestward for over 90 miles through several eastern counties on this Friday afternoon/early evening was likely the local area's most noteworthy individual storm of the 2020 convective season. In addition to dropping three fairly brief EF-0 tornadoes in southeastern Hamilton and northeastern Clay counties, its main calling card was large hail, with several reports of golf ball to slightly larger than baseball size stones in Clay/Nuckolls counties, and smaller hail to around quarter size as far north as Osceola. Interestingly, damaging winds were not a major player, with no ground-truth gusts in excess of 50 MPH. Focusing on specific storm reports, and starting with the three EF-0 tornadoes, they all occurred between 5-6 p.m. CDT, touching down briefly along a roughly 15-mile long corridor from near Interstate 80 a few miles south of Hampton, down to the Saronville area. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving storms led to heavy rain and flash flooding in many places across southeast Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported more county roads being closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms tracked across southeast Nebraska producing damaging wind gusts and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported several large tree limbs down near Roca.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storms moved across eastern Nebraska producing hail and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from mping of 1 inch tree limbs broken and shingles blown off of roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved north and produced a brief tornado and wind damage. A cold front quickly followed the warm front and let to several reports of 50+ mph non-thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A utility company reported hail up to the size of quarters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across eastern Nebraska and produced damaging winds, flash flooding, and a few hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 1 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A confluent trough developed across the Northern Plains and locked in the cold air which had pushed southward and produced numerous record low maximum and minimum temperatures. As an extended period of warm advection and frontogenesis intensified during the afternoon of October 24, a broad area of snowfall developed, with periods of moderate to briefly heavy snowfall during the morning and early afternoon of October 25. Numerous snowfall records were established on October 24th and 25th to go along with the record cold temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.9 inches inches at Concord and 3.0 inches at Newcastle.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.3 inches at Emerson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter weather system impacted the area bringing wide swaths of 5 to 7 inches of snow. Farther to the south, ice accumulated on top of the snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 4.0 inches of rain and accumulating sleet and freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area bringing with it light snow fall and winds gusting up to 60 mph. Blizzard conditions were observed much of the area. Many other locations not in the blizzard warning, experience near blizzard conditions and had impacts warranting a Winter Storm warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observations from ASOS confirmed blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the final week of August 2020, and for the first time since January 2017, a small portion of South Central Nebraska was assigned D2 Severe Drought by the weekly U.S. Drought Monitor. Specifically, D2 was assigned to much of Kearney and far western Adams counties, a bullseye of dryness within larger areas of both D1 Moderate Drought and D0 Abnormally Dry conditions that had gradually expanded in coverage/evolved during the previous few months. This introduction of D2 drought marked a relatively-rapid decline in moisture conditions within South Central Nebraska, as the entire 24-county area began the spring season completely void of all drought categories (including D0), and D1 did not show up within Kearney/Adams counties until July. ||Going into more background detail: the first several months of 2020 started out void of all drought concerns, largely due to a notably-wet 2019. However, during the later spring and early summer, Abnormally Dry (D0) to localized Moderate Drought (D1) began showing up mainly near and south of the Nebraska-Kansas border, while a wet May kept drought at bay across most local Nebraska counties. But then, rainfall patterns became quite interesting and highly-variable during June-July. Over the course of these two months, most northern, western and particularly southern portions of South Central Nebraska received appreciable rainfall (especially during July), while in contrast, a handful of counties in the heart of the area (particularly Kearney/Adams and parts of surrounding) kept missing out on one rain event after another, allowing Moderate Drought (D1) to take hold. Finally, August came around and nearly the ENTIRE 24-county area was dealt well-below-normal rain (most areas only 10-40 percent of the monthly normal). This lack of August rain, in tandem with a notably-hot nine-day stretch centered from Aug. 20-28 (highs in the 90s each day), finally prompted the introduction of this localized Severe Drought (D2). Focusing specifically on the NWS cooperative observer at Minden (the only official NWS precipitation site within the D2 area), there was no sign of trouble as late as May, which was a notably-wet month with 7.91 inches of rain (3.36 above normal). However, the proverbial faucet shut off quite drastically thereafter, as the combined months of June-August only yielded 3.77...merely 34 percent of the normal of 10.99. Fortunately, hot temperatures were not a major contributing drought factor during this 3-month stretch in the heart of the agricultural growing season, as readings only averaged slightly above normal during this time (1.4 degrees above normal at Minden). Given that drought materialized fairly rapidly during the summer and that the first part of the year (and the entirety of the preceding few years) had been rather wet, there were no widespread/serious drought impacts reported in the local area through the end of August, although local Nebraska Extension did note some drought stress in pastures and dryland crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mainly between midnight and 4 a.m. CDT on this early Thursday morning, scattered strong-to-briefly-severe thunderstorms rumbled along a narrow corridor of South Central Nebraska from west-northwest to east-southeast, primarily affecting these counties: Dawson, Buffalo, Hall, Adams, Clay, Fillmore and Thayer. Although radar data suggested that spotty hail to at least nickel size likely occurred within various counties, ground-truth verification was quite limited, consisting of only a few reports of penny to quarter size stones in Hastings around 1:30 a.m. CDT, followed a short time later by the only severe-criteria report of the event: golf ball size hail at Inland in western Clay County. A few hours later (around 4 a.m. CDT), the final strong local storms of the morning dissipated over eastern Fillmore/Thayer counties. There were no reports of strong/severe wind gusts. ||This somewhat-unexpected nocturnal convection (at least in terms of intensity) initiated in the local area over Dawson County shortly before midnight CDT, roughly 50 miles east of a small cluster of strong storms already ongoing off to the west, and roughly 75-100 miles north of the centroid of a small mesoscale convective system (MCS) sliding across northern Kansas (in fact the local storms appeared to be a northward-displaced offshoot of the Kansas MCS). Whether or not the local convection was directly tied to the activity farther south, mesoscale analysis revealed that it was likely sparked by a subtle ribbon of low-mid level warm air/moisture advection within a weak southwesterly low level jet axis (winds at 850 millibars less than 25 knots). Convective parameters were at least marginally-supportive of sporadic intense updrafts, featuring around 1500 J/kg most-unstable CAPE and up to around 30 knots of deep-layer wind shear.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought intense non-thunderstorm and thunderstorm-related wind gusts to south central Nebraska on Sunday evening the 11th. This front entered western portions of the Hastings NWS county warning area (CWA) around 7pm CDT. The cold front already had a history of producing severe caliber, non-thunderstorm, straight line winds over western and central Nebraska, and this trend continued along and just behind the cold front as it marched through south central Nebraska. The strongest winds, initially, were focused roughly along an area from Furnas County northeast into Buffalo County, where measured and estimated wind gusts of 55-65 mph were fairly common. Damage reports from these areas were fairly typical for winds of this magnitude and mainly limited to tree and power line damage. By 7:30pm CDT, an area of showers and thunderstorms developed along and just behind the cold front over Kearney and Franklin Counties as the cold front continued to progress east. This activity reached roughly the Highway 281 corridor from Grand Island to Hastings around 8pm CDT. Wind gusts associated with the passage of the cold front had weakened slightly by this time, but were still quite strong around 50-55 mph, with mainly minor tree damage and power outages reported in the Tri Cities. After 8pm CDT, the area of thunderstorms began to expand northeast and connected up with existing thunderstorms that had already developed along the cold front over northeast and east central Nebraska, essentially filling in a gap that had existed in between the two thunderstorm clusters. Thunderstorm intensity increased just east of Hastings between 8pm CDT and 8:30pm CDT, such that the wind gusts produced by the thunderstorms exacerbated the already-high ambient synoptically driven non-thunderstorm wind gusts associated with the cold front. This resulted in the strongest wind gusts of the evening, generally 60 to 70mph, in a swath that covered portions of Clay, Fillmore, and Thayer Counties. Significant tree damage occurred in these areas, along with damage to a few empty grain bins. Another significant impact, albeit somewhat less obvious or common than typical tree/power line damage, was blowing dust and corn stalks (this event occurred during the harvest season) that led to reduced visibility and ultimately contributed to a four-vehicle accident along Highway 81 just north of Bruning. Another incidence of poor visibility caused a vehicle to leave the roadway in Clay County. ||The highest wind gust with this event was 71 mph, measured by a mesonet station in Thayer County. As mentioned above, the damaging wind events in the eastern half of the CWA were largely driven by convective winds that enhanced already-high ambient winds. Farther west, the severe wind was non-convective and driven by intense pressure rises behind the cold front on the order of 3-5 mb/2 hrs. Overall, this event was one of the more significant wind events of the season and was somewhat unique due to the narrow overlap, spatially and temporally, of both convective and non-convective damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be near 60 MPH caused minor tree damage and downed power lines in Clay Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two separate rounds of mostly non-severe thunderstorms swept through South Central Nebraska from northwest-to-southeast on this Monday afternoon and evening. During the first round, one storm briefly pulsed up to severe levels over Polk County around 4:30 p.m. CDT, as estimated 60 MPH winds felled a large tree branch onto an attached garage in Osceola. Elsewhere in the area, a few near-severe-criteria storm reports included: 54 MPH gusts near Oxford (during the second round of storms), 52 MPH at York airport and penny to nickel size hail in Fillmore County. ||The first round of isolated to scattered convection affected mainly the northeastern one-third of the local area (primarily east of a Greeley-Aurora-Hebron line), first entering counties such as Greeley/Nance between 3-4 p.m. CDT and exiting Fillmore/Thayer counties between 6-7 p.m. CDT. Meanwhile, the second round of storms arrived a bit later and farther west (mainly affecting areas southwest of a Loup City-Hastings-Superior line), entering Dawson/Buffalo counties between 5:30-6:30 p.m. CDT and exiting southward into Kansas between 8-9 p.m. CDT. Long before reaching South Central Nebraska, both rounds of convection initiated well to the north-northwest (near the NE-SD border) early in the afternoon. This was a fairly classic northwest-flow-aloft event, as the Central Plains resided within the path of several small-scale disturbances tracking southeastward around the periphery of a larger-scale trough centered over south central Canada. Despite only modest low-level moisture in the wake of a secondary cold front, this convection made the most of steep low-level lapse rates and seasonably-cool mid level temps, with mid-late afternoon mesoscale parameters in South Central Nebraska featuring 1000-1500 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 25-35 knots of deep-layer wind shear.

A storm system caused winter storm conditions to develop during the overnight hours on October 24th, which continued October 25th into the early morning hours on October 26th. Total snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 9 inches with local amounts a high as 10 to 14 inches from southern Garden County into Deuel County. North to northeast winds of 15 to 25 mph caused some minor blowing and drifting with visibilities as low as a half mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: General public located in Bassett reported 8 inches of snowfall. Snowfall amounts from 4 to 8 inches were common across Rock County. North to northeast winds ranged as high as 15 to 25 mph at times and caused minor blowing and drifting with visibility as low as a half mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two separate rounds of mostly non-severe thunderstorms swept through South Central Nebraska from northwest-to-southeast on this Monday afternoon and evening. During the first round, one storm briefly pulsed up to severe levels over Polk County around 4:30 p.m. CDT, as estimated 60 MPH winds felled a large tree branch onto an attached garage in Osceola. Elsewhere in the area, a few near-severe-criteria storm reports included: 54 MPH gusts near Oxford (during the second round of storms), 52 MPH at York airport and penny to nickel size hail in Fillmore County. ||The first round of isolated to scattered convection affected mainly the northeastern one-third of the local area (primarily east of a Greeley-Aurora-Hebron line), first entering counties such as Greeley/Nance between 3-4 p.m. CDT and exiting Fillmore/Thayer counties between 6-7 p.m. CDT. Meanwhile, the second round of storms arrived a bit later and farther west (mainly affecting areas southwest of a Loup City-Hastings-Superior line), entering Dawson/Buffalo counties between 5:30-6:30 p.m. CDT and exiting southward into Kansas between 8-9 p.m. CDT. Long before reaching South Central Nebraska, both rounds of convection initiated well to the north-northwest (near the NE-SD border) early in the afternoon. This was a fairly classic northwest-flow-aloft event, as the Central Plains resided within the path of several small-scale disturbances tracking southeastward around the periphery of a larger-scale trough centered over south central Canada. Despite only modest low-level moisture in the wake of a secondary cold front, this convection made the most of steep low-level lapse rates and seasonably-cool mid level temps, with mid-late afternoon mesoscale parameters in South Central Nebraska featuring 1000-1500 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 25-35 knots of deep-layer wind shear.

A storm system caused winter storm conditions to develop during the overnight hours on October 24th, which continued October 25th into the early morning hours on October 26th. Total snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 9 inches with local amounts a high as 10 to 14 inches from southern Garden County into Deuel County. North to northeast winds of 15 to 25 mph caused some minor blowing and drifting with visibilities as low as a half mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cooperative observer located 6 miles north of Gordon reported 6.5 inches of snowfall. Snowfall amounts from 6 to 8 inches were common across Sheridan County. North winds ranged as high as 15 to 25 mph at times and caused minor blowing and drifting with visibility as low as a half mile.
