On the evening of Tuesday the 2nd into the very early morning of Wednesday the 3rd, a cluster of strong to briefly-severe thunderstorms rumbled through the southwestern quadrant of South Central Nebraska (primarily affecting counties south of Interstate 80 and west of Highway 281). While most of this activity was sub-severe, a briefly-more-intense storm prompted a lone report of quarter size hail near Stamford around 10:45 p.m. CDT (penny size hail occurred in Beaver City shortly before this). ||Initially, South Central Nebraska remained mostly storm-free during the peak heating of late afternoon/early evening, but not by very far, as counties slightly to the west within southwest Nebraska and northwest Kansas saw a few strong to severe storms develop near a slow-moving cold front. Eventually, between 8-10 p.m. CDT, a loosely-organized cluster of multicell convection that evolved from this early activity propagated east into far southwestern local counties. Then, between 10 p.m. and midnight CDT a few embedded cells intensified enough to prompt severe thunderstorm warnings for parts of Furnas/Harlan/Phelps counties (although the quarter size hail near Stamford was the only ground-truth verification). After midnight, the dominant cell within the cluster gradually weakened as it traversed parts of Franklin/Kearney counties, dissipating by 2 a.m. CDT over western Adams County. While the evening mesoscale environment in South Central Nebraska was obviously sufficient for a marginally-severe storm, it was not supportive of widespread severe activity given that respectable mixed-layer CAPE of 1000-2000 J/kg was offset by weak deep-layer wind shear of no more than 25 knots. In the mid-upper levels, the local area resided under fairly anemic quasi-zonal flow, as the primary upper jet (and resultant stronger shear) resided farther north over the Dakotas.

This Wednesday evening featured the first somewhat-widespread severe thunderstorm event of 2020 within South Central Nebraska, with several reports of damaging winds and large hail focused primarily near and northeast of a line from Loup City-Grand Island-Hebron. Overall, damaging winds were the bigger issue, highlighted by a measured gust of 79 MPH at the York airport shortly before 10 p.m. CDT. As these winds tore into town, they took down several large tree branches (a few entire trees), causing fairly widespread power outages and even partially-crushing an unoccupied car. About an hour before reaching York, measured winds of 70 MPH struck the Chapman area, flipping over irrigation pivots and taking down a light pole at a convenience store. Later in the evening, shortly before storms completely exited the local area, estimated 70 MPH winds took down a giant tree limb in Alexandria (3 ft. diameter/50 ft. long). Although the majority of wind damage focused within eastern counties, severe-criteria gusts were ongoing even as storms first entered the local area earlier in the evening, including 64 MPH at Ord airport. As for hail, the majority of limited reports were quarter size or smaller, although a few stones up to golf ball size were noted in North Loup.||Breaking down event timing and evolution, the scattered to numerous storms that eventually infiltrated South Central Nebraska first developed as separate storm clusters during the mid-late afternoon, one over northwest Nebraska and another in the NE/KS/CO border area. Between 7-9 p.m. CDT, both of these clusters entered northern and western portions of the local area. The southern one, which had been occasionally severe over southwest Nebraska, lost some steam as it moved east, yielding only slightly sub-severe wind gust reports in Furnas County. Meanwhile, the northern storm complex clearly established itself as dominant, charging southeastward as damaging winds peaked in intensity between 8:30-11:30 p.m. CDT. Shortly before midnight, the back edge of the now linear mesoscale convective system (MCS) exited southeast out of Thayer County, ending all storm activity for the night. Turning to the meteorological setup, this was not a strongly-forced event, with the main feature in the mid-upper levels consisting of a low-amplitude shortwave trough tracking east-southeast across Nebraska out of western SD over the course of the afternoon-evening. Surface features across South Central Nebraska ahead of the storms were fairly nondescript, dominated by weak high pressure and southerly-to-easterly breezes only around 10 MPH or less. However, fairly steep low-mid level lapse rates were in place, with damaging wind potential likely enhanced by fairly large surface dewpoint depressions heading into the evening (on the order of 25-30 degrees F). Evening mesoscale analysis over South Central Nebraska revealed 1000-2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and around 35-40 knots of deep-layer wind shear.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation continued below normal across northeast Nebraska, varying from 50 to 75 percent of normal. Temperatures were well-below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate (D1) drought evolved to severe (D2) drought conditions early in the month. Rainfall around 50 percent of normal contributed to the worsening short-term agricultural drought, despite temperatures averaging more than three degrees below normal at Concord. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme and severe drought conditions expanded into additional areas across western and north central Nebraska in October. By the end of the month, severe to extreme drought conditions were present in the eastern Nebraska Panhandle, as well as generally south of Interstate 80 and US Highway 26. Severe drought conditions also expanded into portions of Garfield and Wheeler counties during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the month of October, extreme (D3) drought conditions expanded into the western 2/3rds of Garden County. Severe drought conditions persisted across the eastern third of Garden County through the end of October. The cooperative station at Lisco reported 0.53 inches of precipitation for the month. This was 0.68 inches below the normal of 1.21 inches. The cooperative station at Oshkosh reported 0.57 inches of precipitation which was 0.68 inches below the normal of 1.25 inches. The cooperative station at Crescent Lakes NWR reported 0.50 inches for the month. This was 0.77 inches below the normal of 1.27 inches.

Severe drought conditions also expanded into portions of Garfield and Wheeler counties during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the month of October, extreme (D3) drought conditions expanded to all but the eastern quarter of Deuel County. For the eastern quarter of the county, severe (D2) drought conditions persisted through the end of October.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of October, severe drought (D2) conditions persisted over southwestern Grant County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the month of October, severe drought (D2) conditions persisted across the western half of Arthur County. The cooperative station at Arthur reported 0.48 inches of precipitation for the month. This was 0.83 inches below the normal of 1.31 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the majority of the 24-county South Central Nebraska area, October marked at least the third-consecutive month of below normal precipitation. However, for a relatively-focused zone within the heart of the area this was actually the fifth-straight dry month. As a result, the U.S. Drought Monitor continued an areal-expansion of both D2 Severe and D1 Moderate Drought during October. Focusing first on the D2 situation: as October began, D2 was already assigned to portions of nine counties in the center of the area (including all/nearly all of Kearney and Adams). Then a week later, portions of eight more counties joined the mix, including a westward extension of the initial D2 area to include sizable portions of counties such as Dawson/Gosper, and also a completely separate area of D2 farther north that encompassed the vast majority of Valley, Greeley, Sherman and Howard counties. Later in the month, a southward expansion of the southern D2 area brought most of Furnas County into the fold, resulting in a total of 18 counties at least partially-assigned Severe Drought by the end of the month. More specifically, the geographic coverage of D2 in South Central Nebraska ballooned from 9 to 37 percent during October. Outside of the worst-off D2 area, both D1 Moderate Drought and D0 Abnormally Dry also increased in coverage, with D1 introduced to all/most of York and Fillmore counties and D0 expanding to include the remainder of Thayer/Nuckolls counties. In other words, the entire 24-county area was assigned some U.S. Drought Monitor category by month's end. ||Taking a closer look at October precipitation, needless to say it was a notably-dry month: not only was the vast majority of the area below normal, but most of it tallied merely 10-50 percent of normal (normals generally 1.50-2.00). The overall-driest conditions concentrated near/south of Interstate 80, where NWS observer totals included a paltry 0.08 at Nelson, 0.11 eight miles south of Elwood (and also at Ravenna and Superior) and 0.12 at Red Cloud and Hebron. On the wetter side of things (but still below normal), parts of mainly far northern counties (such as Valley/Greeley) measured as much as 1.00-1.50, thanks mainly to early-season snowfall. Over the longer-term five-month period covering June-October, much of particularly the D2 areas only totaled 5-8 (generally 30-50% of normal). A few of the very driest NWS stations during this stretch included: Minden 5.08 (driest on record), Kearney airport 5.14 (3rd-driest), Hastings airport 5.87 (driest on record) and St. Paul 6.28 (3rd-driest). Temperature-wise, although October as a whole averaged 2-4 degrees F cooler-than-normal across South Central Nebraska (thanks mainly to a very cold late-month stretch), the first part of the month featured a blast of early-fall heat as highs in most places soared well into the 80s for a full week between the 5th-11th. Although no widespread/major drought impacts were noted locally during October, one indicator of the increasingly-dry conditions unfolded on the evening of the 11th as high winds prompted areas of blowing dust, greatly-reducing visibility and reportedly contributing to at least a few traffic accidents.

Then a week later, portions of eight more counties joined the mix, including a westward extension of the initial D2 area to include sizable portions of counties such as Dawson/Gosper, and also a completely separate area of D2 farther north that encompassed the vast majority of Valley, Greeley, Sherman and Howard counties. Later in the month, a southward expansion of the southern D2 area brought most of Furnas County into the fold, resulting in a total of 18 counties at least partially-assigned Severe Drought by the end of the month. More specifically, the geographic coverage of D2 in South Central Nebraska ballooned from 9 to 37 percent during October. Outside of the worst-off D2 area, both D1 Moderate Drought and D0 Abnormally Dry also increased in coverage, with D1 introduced to all/most of York and Fillmore counties and D0 expanding to include the remainder of Thayer/Nuckolls counties. In other words, the entire 24-county area was assigned some U.S. Drought Monitor category by month's end. ||Taking a closer look at October precipitation, needless to say it was a notably-dry month: not only was the vast majority of the area below normal, but most of it tallied merely 10-50 percent of normal (normals generally 1.50-2.00). The overall-driest conditions concentrated near/south of Interstate 80, where NWS observer totals included a paltry 0.08 at Nelson, 0.11 eight miles south of Elwood (and also at Ravenna and Superior) and 0.12 at Red Cloud and Hebron. On the wetter side of things (but still below normal), parts of mainly far northern counties (such as Valley/Greeley) measured as much as 1.00-1.50, thanks mainly to early-season snowfall. Over the longer-term five-month period covering June-October, much of particularly the D2 areas only totaled 5-8 (generally 30-50% of normal). A few of the very driest NWS stations during this stretch included: Minden 5.08 (driest on record), Kearney airport 5.14 (3rd-driest), Hastings airport 5.87 (driest on record) and St. Paul 6.28 (3rd-driest). Temperature-wise, although October as a whole averaged 2-4 degrees F cooler-than-normal across South Central Nebraska (thanks mainly to a very cold late-month stretch), the first part of the month featured a blast of early-fall heat as highs in most places soared well into the 80s for a full week between the 5th-11th. Although no widespread/major drought impacts were noted locally during October, one indicator of the increasingly-dry conditions unfolded on the evening of the 11th as high winds prompted areas of blowing dust, greatly-reducing visibility and reportedly contributing to at least a few traffic accidents.

This Wednesday evening featured the first somewhat-widespread severe thunderstorm event of 2020 within South Central Nebraska, with several reports of damaging winds and large hail focused primarily near and northeast of a line from Loup City-Grand Island-Hebron. Overall, damaging winds were the bigger issue, highlighted by a measured gust of 79 MPH at the York airport shortly before 10 p.m. CDT. As these winds tore into town, they took down several large tree branches (a few entire trees), causing fairly widespread power outages and even partially-crushing an unoccupied car. About an hour before reaching York, measured winds of 70 MPH struck the Chapman area, flipping over irrigation pivots and taking down a light pole at a convenience store. Later in the evening, shortly before storms completely exited the local area, estimated 70 MPH winds took down a giant tree limb in Alexandria (3 ft. diameter/50 ft. long). Although the majority of wind damage focused within eastern counties, severe-criteria gusts were ongoing even as storms first entered the local area earlier in the evening, including 64 MPH at Ord airport. As for hail, the majority of limited reports were quarter size or smaller, although a few stones up to golf ball size were noted in North Loup.||Breaking down event timing and evolution, the scattered to numerous storms that eventually infiltrated South Central Nebraska first developed as separate storm clusters during the mid-late afternoon, one over northwest Nebraska and another in the NE/KS/CO border area. Between 7-9 p.m. CDT, both of these clusters entered northern and western portions of the local area. The southern one, which had been occasionally severe over southwest Nebraska, lost some steam as it moved east, yielding only slightly sub-severe wind gust reports in Furnas County. Meanwhile, the northern storm complex clearly established itself as dominant, charging southeastward as damaging winds peaked in intensity between 8:30-11:30 p.m. CDT. Shortly before midnight, the back edge of the now linear mesoscale convective system (MCS) exited southeast out of Thayer County, ending all storm activity for the night. Turning to the meteorological setup, this was not a strongly-forced event, with the main feature in the mid-upper levels consisting of a low-amplitude shortwave trough tracking east-southeast across Nebraska out of western SD over the course of the afternoon-evening. Surface features across South Central Nebraska ahead of the storms were fairly nondescript, dominated by weak high pressure and southerly-to-easterly breezes only around 10 MPH or less. However, fairly steep low-mid level lapse rates were in place, with damaging wind potential likely enhanced by fairly large surface dewpoint depressions heading into the evening (on the order of 25-30 degrees F). Evening mesoscale analysis over South Central Nebraska revealed 1000-2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and around 35-40 knots of deep-layer wind shear.

Albeit not as active as the previous evening, the evening of Thursday the 4th was the third consecutive with at least some severe storm activity in South Central Nebraska. This was truly a hit-and-miss, isolated to scattered event, as while storm coverage was fairly limited across the 24-county area as a whole, all four quadrants of it saw at least some convection. Overall, marginally-severe winds were the main issue, including measured gusts of 61 MPH at the Ord airport and 60 MPH at Waco, and estimated 60 MPH at Shelby, where the NWS cooperative observer reported downed tree branches up to 3 inches in diameter. Various other places clocked slightly sub-severe wind gusts of 50-55 MPH, including in or near Gothenburg, Johnson Lake, Arcadia, Gresham and Clay Center. Hail was less of an issue, with the only severe-criteria report consisting of quarter size stones in Sutton. ||Breaking down event evolution and timing, two small/separate storm clusters entered west-central and northern counties between 7-9 p.m. CDT, a continuation of activity that initiated hours earlier over the Nebraska Panhandle. These early evening storms produced strong-to-marginally severe winds mainly in Valley/Dawson/Gosper counties. Over the next few hours from 9-11:30 p.m. CDT, both of these storm clusters briefly weakened, only to re-intensify and even merge somewhat as they reached eastern portions of the area, prompting various hail/wind reports from counties such as Clay/Polk/York before exiting into eastern Nebraska. Meanwhile, back off to the southwest, a separate area of strong storms blossomed between 10 p.m. and midnight CDT in several counties along the Kansas border, but there were no ground-truth reports from this convection that departed into southeast Nebraska/northeast Kansas after midnight. Finally, one last weaker batch of storms roamed various northern/eastern counties between 1-4 a.m. CDT on Friday the 5th before all local storm activity ended for the night. Both at the surface and aloft, the same general environment that supported severe storms the prior evening remained in place for this round. In the mid-upper levels,  broad west-northwest flow containing various embedded, low-amplitude disturbances persisted over the region. At the surface, the same seasonably-hot and moderately unstable airmass persisted as well, as afternoon temperatures topped out in the low-mid 90s F with dewpoints ranging from upper 50s F in western counties to 65-70 F in the east. As strong to severe storms traversed the area in the evening, mesoscale parameters featured 1000-3000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 40 knots of deep-layer wind shear.

At the surface, the same seasonably-hot and moderately unstable airmass persisted as well, as afternoon temperatures topped out in the low-mid 90s F with dewpoints ranging from upper 50s F in western counties to 65-70 F in the east. As strong to severe storms traversed the area in the evening, mesoscale parameters featured 1000-3000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 40 knots of deep-layer wind shear.

Albeit not as active as the previous evening, the evening of Thursday the 4th was the third consecutive with at least some severe storm activity in South Central Nebraska. This was truly a hit-and-miss, isolated to scattered event, as while storm coverage was fairly limited across the 24-county area as a whole, all four quadrants of it saw at least some convection. Overall, marginally-severe winds were the main issue, including measured gusts of 61 MPH at the Ord airport and 60 MPH at Waco, and estimated 60 MPH at Shelby, where the NWS cooperative observer reported downed tree branches up to 3 inches in diameter. Various other places clocked slightly sub-severe wind gusts of 50-55 MPH, including in or near Gothenburg, Johnson Lake, Arcadia, Gresham and Clay Center. Hail was less of an issue, with the only severe-criteria report consisting of quarter size stones in Sutton. ||Breaking down event evolution and timing, two small/separate storm clusters entered west-central and northern counties between 7-9 p.m. CDT, a continuation of activity that initiated hours earlier over the Nebraska Panhandle. These early evening storms produced strong-to-marginally severe winds mainly in Valley/Dawson/Gosper counties. Over the next few hours from 9-11:30 p.m. CDT, both of these storm clusters briefly weakened, only to re-intensify and even merge somewhat as they reached eastern portions of the area, prompting various hail/wind reports from counties such as Clay/Polk/York before exiting into eastern Nebraska. Meanwhile, back off to the southwest, a separate area of strong storms blossomed between 10 p.m. and midnight CDT in several counties along the Kansas border, but there were no ground-truth reports from this convection that departed into southeast Nebraska/northeast Kansas after midnight. Finally, one last weaker batch of storms roamed various northern/eastern counties between 1-4 a.m. CDT on Friday the 5th before all local storm activity ended for the night. Both at the surface and aloft, the same general environment that supported severe storms the prior evening remained in place for this round. In the mid-upper levels,  broad west-northwest flow containing various embedded, low-amplitude disturbances persisted over the region. At the surface, the same seasonably-hot and moderately unstable airmass persisted as well, as afternoon temperatures topped out in the low-mid 90s F with dewpoints ranging from upper 50s F in western counties to 65-70 F in the east. As strong to severe storms traversed the area in the evening, mesoscale parameters featured 1000-3000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 40 knots of deep-layer wind shear.

It's quite rare that widespread/severe NON-thunderstorm winds take center stage over thunderstorms during June in South Central Nebraska, but that's exactly what happened on this Tuesday as an anomalously strong low pressure system (by June standards) churned through the Central Plains. For the vast majority of the local area, the number one story was several hours of intense north winds, commonly sustained 30-40 MPH and gusting 40-60+ MPH in the wake of a powerful late-spring cold front. Based on a variety of official/unofficial sensors, some of the highest afternoon-evening gusts featured: 67 MPH in Doniphan, 65 MPH near Gothenburg, Roseland and Oxford, and 64 MPH at Central Nebraska Regional Airport in Grand Island. Other official peak airport gusts included 62 MPH at Hastings and 60 MPH in Kearney.  These winds undoubtedly caused numerous instances of at least minor damage within the area, but a few known ones included: a large tree in Holdrege fell on a car and blocked a street; a few large branches in west Hastings crashed onto power lines and at least one house; downed power lines in Arapahoe and Harvard; 6-8 inch diameter tree limbs downed in Deshler. Although the overall-strongest winds started/ended several hours earlier in far western local counties compared to far eastern counties, for the majority of the area the worst winds concentrated during portions of the noon-10 p.m. CDT time frame.||Turning to thunderstorms, while non-severe activity was common across much of South Central Nebraska during the afternoon and evening, the vast majority of the area was spared severe storms. This was mainly due to the strong surface cold front passing through prior to robust development being able to get going (in fact most of the area had seasonably-cool temperatures in the 50s-60s during the afternoon). That being said, a few severe storms did flare up in eastern counties during the afternoon, including one elevated hailer that dropped stones to around golf ball size in the Henderson and Hampton areas. Farther southeast along the actual front, radar indicated severe wind/hail potential with near-surface-based storms in/near Thayer County, but there were no verifying reports (although gustnado activity was noted in far southeastern Thayer County). Rainfall-wise, 24-hour totals fell short of forecast expectations across the majority of the area, as most places measured less than 1 inch. However, a few localized pockets closer to 2 inches occurred primarily in portions of southern Thayer and southwestern Buffalo counties, including a very isolated bullseye of 2.80 at Hubbell.||Both in the mid-upper levels and at the surface, the intensity of this weather system was quite abnormal for June...more reminiscent of those that sometimes occur earlier in the spring. Aloft, a deep closed low slowly tracked from eastern CO around daybreak on the 9th to northeast KS by sunset, eventually departing the Central Plains into northern MO by sunrise on the 10th. The associated surface low center lumbered east across northern KS over the course of the day, deepening to around 993 millibars in the process. While the position and track of the surface low/cold front spared most of the area from severe storms, far southeast counties dodged a potentially significant threat during the afternoon as the environment just north of the northern KS surface low became conditionally supportive of supercells and possible tornadoes. In fact, mesoscale parameters in the Thayer County area briefly featured a potent combination of mixed-layer CAPE around 2000 J/kg, deep layer wind shear of 50-70 knots and 0-1 kilometer shear of 20-30 knots. As for the unusual nature of such intense northerly winds so late in the spring, it's worth noting that howling speeds of 50-70 knots a few thousand feet above the ground (at 850 millibars) registered 3-4 standard deviations above normal for the date...basically off the charts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report received via mPING.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme and severe drought conditions expanded into additional areas across western and north central Nebraska in October. By the end of the month, severe to extreme drought conditions were present in the eastern Nebraska Panhandle, as well as generally south of Interstate 80 and US Highway 26. Severe drought conditions also expanded into portions of Garfield and Wheeler counties during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the month of October, severe drought (D2) conditions persisted across all of Keith County. The cooperative observer at Kingsley Dam reported 0.47 inches of precipitation. This was 0.80 inches below the normal of 1.27 inches. The cooperative station at Ogallala reported 0.25 inches which was 1.02 inches below the normal of 1.27 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the month of October, severe drought (D2) conditions continued across all of Perkins County.

Rainfall-wise, 24-hour totals fell short of forecast expectations across the majority of the area, as most places measured less than 1 inch. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme and severe drought conditions expanded into additional areas across western and north central Nebraska in October. By the end of the month, severe to extreme drought conditions were present in the eastern Nebraska Panhandle, as well as generally south of Interstate 80 and US Highway 26. Severe drought conditions also expanded into portions of Garfield and Wheeler counties during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the month of October, severe drought (D2) conditions expanded into southeastern and east central Lincoln County. The cooperative station at Wallace 2W reported 0.24 inches of precipitation. This was 1.35 inches below the normal of 1.59 inches. The cooperative station at Hershey 5SSE reported 0.26 inches of precipitation in October. This was 1.40 inches below the normal of 1.66 inches. At the North Platte Airport, 0.38 inches of precipitation fell which was 1.17 inches below the normal of 1.55 inches. At the cooperative station in Brady, 0.24 inches of precipitation fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across portions of south central Nebraska during the evening of Tuesday, June 30th, along and just behind a cold front. Some of these thunderstorms became severe, bringing scattered instances of large hail, along with isolated severe wind. The first thunderstorm formed over Dawson County, just behind the cold front, around 8pm, and produced quarter size hail just north of Sumner. This storm continued to produce severe hail as it moved northeast into Custer County between 8pm and 9pm. A more extensive line of thunderstorms developed farther east around 8:30pm, from eastern Buffalo County northeast to Greeley County. This line moved east southeast. Southern portions of the line were the most intense and produced a swath of large hail reports ranging from quarter size up to half dollar size from Cairo into Grand Island. A severe wind gust of 62 mph was also measured at Central Nebraska Regional Airport (KGRI). The line of thunderstorms continued east southeast, affecting areas east of Highway 281 and north of Highway 6 between 9:45pm and midnight. However, no additional severe reports were received within these areas during this time.||The thunderstorms were supported by strong instability (mixed layer CAPE values of 4500-5500 J/kg) and modest effective shear (25-35kt). Steep mid level lapse rates near 8 C/km and a glancing influence from a progressive shortwave moving from Colorado into Nebraska Panhandle also aided in severe thunderstorm formation, though the position of this upper disturbance  kept the greatest deep layer shear displaced northwest from the cold front and strongest instability. The low level jet remained rather weak, thus the line of thunderstorms decreased in intensity after sunset with the onset of nocturnal cooling.

Across western and north central Nebrasaka in November, precipitation was below normal for the month of November. Conditions were driest in the eastern Nebraska Panhandle, western Sandhills, and southwestern Nebraska.  Locations in the eastern Sandhills and portions of central Nebraska had near normal to slightly below normal precipitation for the month. By the end of the month, extreme (D3) drought conditions had expanded into most of southwestern Nebraska. Temperatures were well above normal for November as Imperial recorded its 3rd warmest November on record. Broken Bow recorded its 4th warmest November and North Platte its 9th warmest November on record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions remained unchanged across Deuel county in November. The eastern quarter of the county remained in severe (D2) drought, while the western 3/4ths of the county remained in extreme (D3) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Across western and north central Nebrasaka in November, precipitation was below normal for the month of November. Conditions were driest in the eastern Nebraska Panhandle, western Sandhills, and southwestern Nebraska.  Locations in the eastern Sandhills and portions of central Nebraska had near normal to slightly below normal precipitation for the month. By the end of the month, extreme (D3) drought conditions had expanded into most of southwestern Nebraska. Temperatures were well above normal for November as Imperial recorded its 3rd warmest November on record. Broken Bow recorded its 4th warmest November and North Platte its 9th warmest November on record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the month of November, drought severity remained unchanged across Sheridan County.  The western half of the county was in extreme (D3) drought with the eastern half of the county in severe (D2) drought. The cooperative station at Gordon 6N reported 0.41 inches of precipitation for the month. This was 0.30 inches below the normal of 0.71 inches. The cooperative station at Ellsworth 2W reported 0.21 inches of precipitation for the month. This was 0.25 inches below the normal of 0.46 inches.

The cooperative station at Oshkosh reported 0.25 inches of precipitation which was 0.25 inches below the normal of 0.50 inches. The cooperative station at Crescent Lakes NWR reported 0.22 inches for the month. This was 0.38 inches below the normal of 0.60 inches. The cooperative station at Oshkosh 8SW reported 0.26 inches of precipitation for the month. This was 0.39 inches below the normal of 0.65 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the month of November, extreme (D3) drought conditions expanded into the southern half of Lincoln county. Along the Platte River Valley in central Lincoln county, severe (D2) drought conditions expanded slightly during the month into east central portions of the county. The cooperative station at Wallace 2W reported 0.23 inches of precipitation for November. This was 0.41 inches below the normal of 0.64 inches. The cooperative station at Hershey 5SSE reported 0.34 inches of precipitation in November. This was 0.22 inches below the normal of 0.56 inches. At the North Platte Airport, 0.28 inches of precipitation fell which was 0.36 inches below the normal of 0.64 inches. At the cooperative station in Brady, 0.16 inches of precipitation fell. Temperatures were well above normal for the month as North Platte recorded its 9th warmest November on record. On the 6th, the high in North Platte was 87 degrees. This broke the all time record high for the month of November.

The derecho that was moving through southeast Wyoming and northeast Colorado continued across the Nebraska Panhandle with severe and significant wind gusts. Received numerous reports of sheds blown over, metal roofs ripped off outbuildings, and large trees uprooted. Two injuries were reported as metal siding was blown across a highway striking two motorcyclists.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS station KSNY - Sidney Municipal Airport reported wind gust in stratiform region of squall line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Conditions with temperatures at record or near-record warm levels on December 22 underwent a drastic change over a brief 24 hour period, as a sharp upper-level trough in westerly flow amplified while translating from the Pacific Northwest to the Plains. Low pressure developed and deepened rapidly while moving from South Dakota around 0000CST December 23 to the northern Great Lakes by 0600CST December 24.||Very steep lower to mid-level lapse rates and lift focused within a 200-250hPa deep layer supportive of effective dendritic growth resulted in intense precipitation banding over a 5 to 8 hour period. Winds in the lower mixed layer reached 40 to 60 knots as Arctic air surged into the area behind the system, producing numerous hours of widespread whiteout conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted as high as 47 mph at South Sioux City, and despite a lack of pre-storm snow cover, snowfall which accumulated to 3.0 inches at Sioux City and 1.5 inches at Emerson resulted in widespread whiteout conditions for several hours. Travel was not advised during the blizzard across all of northeast NE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thanks to a somewhat-unexpectedly potent combination of intense northwest winds and relatively-minimal snow, mainly the daytime hours on this Wednesday featured several hours of near-blizzard to outright-blizzard conditions across most of South Central Nebraska, shutting down several highways for a time and snarling pre-Christmas travel. Various law enforcement agencies reported dozens of traffic accidents and slide-offs (fortunately none fatal within the local area) and the following main highways were reported closed at various times: Interstate 80 between York and Kearney, Highway 281 between Grand Island and Hastings, Highway 30 from Grand Island-Kearney, Highway 34 from Grand Island-York, Highway 6 from Hastings-Kenesaw and Highway 81 near Shelby (all had re-opened by late afternoon). In Adams County, snowplows were pulled off the road for several hours due to very poor visibility. As for areal coverage of white-out conditions, the worst/longest-lasting issues focused within counties both along/north of Highway 6 and also along/east of Highway 183 (including the Tri Cities). Within this area, most places endured at least semi-frequent blizzard conditions for 6-8 hours while accumulating 1-3 of snow, while far northern counties (such as Valley/Greeley) saw blizzard conditions for as long as 10-12 hours and received slightly-higher snow amounts up to 3-4. Meanwhile, the situation was not as severe within far southern/southwest counties especially near the Kansas border, where snow was lighter/less-intense compared to farther north (amounts mainly under 1) and didn't last as long. Although the magnitude of wintry precipitation/blizzard impacts varied somewhat across South Central Nebraska, all 24 counties dealt with at least 9-12 hours of feisty northwest winds, commonly sustained 30-40 MPH and gusting 45-60+ MPH. A few of the highest official peak gusts at airports included: 76 MPH at Ord and 61 MPH at Grand Island, Hastings and York. While there were no known reports of substantial wind damage, NWS observers near both Arcadia and Shelby noted that several small tree branches were downed. ||Getting into more detail regarding event timing and meteorological background, the main driving force in the mid-upper levels was the passage of a deep, slightly-positively-tilted trough, with its primary vorticity maximum tracking from the Central Rockies on the evening of the 22nd, and then across the heart of Nebraska-Kansas during the day on the 23rd before departing into the Upper Midwest/Upper Great Lakes through the evening-overnight. At the surface, the associated low pressure center gradually intensified from around 994 millibars as it crossed Nebraska during the overnight-early morning of the 22nd-23rd, to 988 millibars by mid-day in the IA-MN-WI border area, and then maintained intensity while reaching Lake Superior overnight. Locally, the initial surge of strong northwest winds crossed South Central Nebraska during the pre-dawn of the 23rd behind the main synoptic cold front, but gusts largely held under 50 MPH. The overall-strongest winds ended up occurring between mid-morning and mid-afternoon, corresponding to the peak surface pressure gradient between the departing/deepening low and strong high pressure nosing in from the west-northwest (for a few hours the west-to-east gradient across the state of Nebraska averaged near 25 millibars!) As for snowfall timing, an initial, narrow band swept across most of the area during the pre-dawn hours, but the primary, broad zone of mainly light-to-moderate snow (with brief heavier bursts) gradually overspread South Central Nebraska from west-northwest to east-southeast during the morning, eventually departing in similar fashion during the afternoon. Actual falling/accumulating snow ended across most of the area between 2-4 p.m., but lingering blowing snow and ground blizzard conditions held on for a few more hours...until gusts diminished more consistently to around 30 MPH-or-less after nightfall. From a forecast review perspective, the intense winds and at least minor blowing snow impacts were fairly well-anticipated, as a High Wind Warning was issued for the entire area 12-18 hours in advance and a Winter Weather Advisory (for the majority of the area) was issued 6-18 hours out. However, especially the southward extent of accumulating snow and the resultant potential for all-out blizzard conditions was not as well foreseen (especially as far south as the Interstate 80/Highway 6 corridors), with several counties not placed under official Blizzard Warnings until rather late in the game. For sure, one of the biggest takeaways/reminders from this event was that even minimal snowfall can wreak havoc on travel in the presence of powerful winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on surrounding observations, periodic gusts near 60 MPH and sustained speeds near 40 MPH occurred across the county. The intense winds, combined with snow, promoted blizzard conditions.

While there were no known reports of substantial wind damage, NWS observers near both Arcadia and Shelby noted that several small tree branches were downed. ||Getting into more detail regarding event timing and meteorological background, the main driving force in the mid-upper levels was the passage of a deep, slightly-positively-tilted trough, with its primary vorticity maximum tracking from the Central Rockies on the evening of the 22nd, and then across the heart of Nebraska-Kansas during the day on the 23rd before departing into the Upper Midwest/Upper Great Lakes through the evening-overnight. Actual falling/accumulating snow ended across most of the area between 2-4 p.m., but lingering blowing snow and ground blizzard conditions held on for a few more hours...until gusts diminished more consistently to around 30 MPH-or-less after nightfall. Meanwhile, the situation was not as severe within far southern/southwest counties especially near the Kansas border, where snow was lighter/less-intense compared to farther north (amounts mainly under 1) and didn't last as long. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Conditions with temperatures at record or near-record warm levels on December 22 underwent a drastic change over a brief 24 hour period, as a sharp upper-level trough in westerly flow amplified while translating from the Pacific Northwest to the Plains. Low pressure developed and deepened rapidly while moving from South Dakota around 0000CST December 23 to the northern Great Lakes by 0600CST December 24.||Very steep lower to mid-level lapse rates and lift focused within a 200-250hPa deep layer supportive of effective dendritic growth resulted in intense precipitation banding over a 5 to 8 hour period. Winds in the lower mixed layer reached 40 to 60 knots as Arctic air surged into the area behind the system, producing numerous hours of widespread whiteout conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted as high as 52 mph 2 miles east of Concord, and despite a lack of pre-storm snow cover, snowfall which accumulated to 3.5 inches at Concord resulted in widespread whiteout conditions for several hours. Travel was not advised during the blizzard across all of northeast NE.

While there were no known reports of substantial wind damage, NWS observers near both Arcadia and Shelby noted that several small tree branches were downed. ||Getting into more detail regarding event timing and meteorological background, the main driving force in the mid-upper levels was the passage of a deep, slightly-positively-tilted trough, with its primary vorticity maximum tracking from the Central Rockies on the evening of the 22nd, and then across the heart of Nebraska-Kansas during the day on the 23rd before departing into the Upper Midwest/Upper Great Lakes through the evening-overnight. At the surface, the associated low pressure center gradually intensified from around 994 millibars as it crossed Nebraska during the overnight-early morning of the 22nd-23rd, to 988 millibars by mid-day in the IA-MN-WI border area, and then maintained intensity while reaching Lake Superior overnight. Locally, the initial surge of strong northwest winds crossed South Central Nebraska during the pre-dawn of the 23rd behind the main synoptic cold front, but gusts largely held under 50 MPH. The overall-strongest winds ended up occurring between mid-morning and mid-afternoon, corresponding to the peak surface pressure gradient between the departing/deepening low and strong high pressure nosing in from the west-northwest (for a few hours the west-to-east gradient across the state of Nebraska averaged near 25 millibars!) As for snowfall timing, an initial, narrow band swept across most of the area during the pre-dawn hours, but the primary, broad zone of mainly light-to-moderate snow (with brief heavier bursts) gradually overspread South Central Nebraska from west-northwest to east-southeast during the morning, eventually departing in similar fashion during the afternoon. Actual falling/accumulating snow ended across most of the area between 2-4 p.m., but lingering blowing snow and ground blizzard conditions held on for a few more hours...until gusts diminished more consistently to around 30 MPH-or-less after nightfall. From a forecast review perspective, the intense winds and at least minor blowing snow impacts were fairly well-anticipated, as a High Wind Warning was issued for the entire area 12-18 hours in advance and a Winter Weather Advisory (for the majority of the area) was issued 6-18 hours out. However, especially the southward extent of accumulating snow and the resultant potential for all-out blizzard conditions was not as well foreseen (especially as far south as the Interstate 80/Highway 6 corridors), with several counties not placed under official Blizzard Warnings until rather late in the game. For sure, one of the biggest takeaways/reminders from this event was that even minimal snowfall can wreak havoc on travel in the presence of powerful winds.

The overall-strongest winds ended up occurring between mid-morning and mid-afternoon, corresponding to the peak surface pressure gradient between the departing/deepening low and strong high pressure nosing in from the west-northwest (for a few hours the west-to-east gradient across the state of Nebraska averaged near 25 millibars!) As for snowfall timing, an initial, narrow band swept across most of the area during the pre-dawn hours, but the primary, broad zone of mainly light-to-moderate snow (with brief heavier bursts) gradually overspread South Central Nebraska from west-northwest to east-southeast during the morning, eventually departing in similar fashion during the afternoon. Actual falling/accumulating snow ended across most of the area between 2-4 p.m., but lingering blowing snow and ground blizzard conditions held on for a few more hours...until gusts diminished more consistently to around 30 MPH-or-less after nightfall. From a forecast review perspective, the intense winds and at least minor blowing snow impacts were fairly well-anticipated, as a High Wind Warning was issued for the entire area 12-18 hours in advance and a Winter Weather Advisory (for the majority of the area) was issued 6-18 hours out. However, especially the southward extent of accumulating snow and the resultant potential for all-out blizzard conditions was not as well foreseen (especially as far south as the Interstate 80/Highway 6 corridors), with several counties not placed under official Blizzard Warnings until rather late in the game. For sure, one of the biggest takeaways/reminders from this event was that even minimal snowfall can wreak havoc on travel in the presence of powerful winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on surrounding observations, periodic gusts near 60 MPH and sustained speeds near 40 MPH occurred across the county. The intense winds, combined with light snow, resulted in near-blizzard conditions at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thanks to a somewhat-unexpectedly potent combination of intense northwest winds and relatively-minimal snow, mainly the daytime hours on this Wednesday featured several hours of near-blizzard to outright-blizzard conditions across most of South Central Nebraska, shutting down several highways for a time and snarling pre-Christmas travel. Various law enforcement agencies reported dozens of traffic accidents and slide-offs (fortunately none fatal within the local area) and the following main highways were reported closed at various times: Interstate 80 between York and Kearney, Highway 281 between Grand Island and Hastings, Highway 30 from Grand Island-Kearney, Highway 34 from Grand Island-York, Highway 6 from Hastings-Kenesaw and Highway 81 near Shelby (all had re-opened by late afternoon). In Adams County, snowplows were pulled off the road for several hours due to very poor visibility. As for areal coverage of white-out conditions, the worst/longest-lasting issues focused within counties both along/north of Highway 6 and also along/east of Highway 183 (including the Tri Cities). Within this area, most places endured at least semi-frequent blizzard conditions for 6-8 hours while accumulating 1-3 of snow, while far northern counties (such as Valley/Greeley) saw blizzard conditions for as long as 10-12 hours and received slightly-higher snow amounts up to 3-4. Meanwhile, the situation was not as severe within far southern/southwest counties especially near the Kansas border, where snow was lighter/less-intense compared to farther north (amounts mainly under 1) and didn't last as long. Although the magnitude of wintry precipitation/blizzard impacts varied somewhat across South Central Nebraska, all 24 counties dealt with at least 9-12 hours of feisty northwest winds, commonly sustained 30-40 MPH and gusting 45-60+ MPH. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thanks to a somewhat-unexpectedly potent combination of intense northwest winds and relatively-minimal snow, mainly the daytime hours on this Wednesday featured several hours of near-blizzard to outright-blizzard conditions across most of South Central Nebraska, shutting down several highways for a time and snarling pre-Christmas travel. Various law enforcement agencies reported dozens of traffic accidents and slide-offs (fortunately none fatal within the local area) and the following main highways were reported closed at various times: Interstate 80 between York and Kearney, Highway 281 between Grand Island and Hastings, Highway 30 from Grand Island-Kearney, Highway 34 from Grand Island-York, Highway 6 from Hastings-Kenesaw and Highway 81 near Shelby (all had re-opened by late afternoon). In Adams County, snowplows were pulled off the road for several hours due to very poor visibility. As for areal coverage of white-out conditions, the worst/longest-lasting issues focused within counties both along/north of Highway 6 and also along/east of Highway 183 (including the Tri Cities). Within this area, most places endured at least semi-frequent blizzard conditions for 6-8 hours while accumulating 1-3 of snow, while far northern counties (such as Valley/Greeley) saw blizzard conditions for as long as 10-12 hours and received slightly-higher snow amounts up to 3-4. Meanwhile, the situation was not as severe within far southern/southwest counties especially near the Kansas border, where snow was lighter/less-intense compared to farther north (amounts mainly under 1) and didn't last as long. Although the magnitude of wintry precipitation/blizzard impacts varied somewhat across South Central Nebraska, all 24 counties dealt with at least 9-12 hours of feisty northwest winds, commonly sustained 30-40 MPH and gusting 45-60+ MPH. A few of the highest official peak gusts at airports included: 76 MPH at Ord and 61 MPH at Grand Island, Hastings and York. While there were no known reports of substantial wind damage, NWS observers near both Arcadia and Shelby noted that several small tree branches were downed. ||Getting into more detail regarding event timing and meteorological background, the main driving force in the mid-upper levels was the passage of a deep, slightly-positively-tilted trough, with its primary vorticity maximum tracking from the Central Rockies on the evening of the 22nd, and then across the heart of Nebraska-Kansas during the day on the 23rd before departing into the Upper Midwest/Upper Great Lakes through the evening-overnight. At the surface, the associated low pressure center gradually intensified from around 994 millibars as it crossed Nebraska during the overnight-early morning of the 22nd-23rd, to 988 millibars by mid-day in the IA-MN-WI border area, and then maintained intensity while reaching Lake Superior overnight. Locally, the initial surge of strong northwest winds crossed South Central Nebraska during the pre-dawn of the 23rd behind the main synoptic cold front, but gusts largely held under 50 MPH. The overall-strongest winds ended up occurring between mid-morning and mid-afternoon, corresponding to the peak surface pressure gradient between the departing/deepening low and strong high pressure nosing in from the west-northwest (for a few hours the west-to-east gradient across the state of Nebraska averaged near 25 millibars!) As for snowfall timing, an initial, narrow band swept across most of the area during the pre-dawn hours, but the primary, broad zone of mainly light-to-moderate snow (with brief heavier bursts) gradually overspread South Central Nebraska from west-northwest to east-southeast during the morning, eventually departing in similar fashion during the afternoon. Actual falling/accumulating snow ended across most of the area between 2-4 p.m., but lingering blowing snow and ground blizzard conditions held on for a few more hours...until gusts diminished more consistently to around 30 MPH-or-less after nightfall. From a forecast review perspective, the intense winds and at least minor blowing snow impacts were fairly well-anticipated, as a High Wind Warning was issued for the entire area 12-18 hours in advance and a Winter Weather Advisory (for the majority of the area) was issued 6-18 hours out. However, especially the southward extent of accumulating snow and the resultant potential for all-out blizzard conditions was not as well foreseen (especially as far south as the Interstate 80/Highway 6 corridors), with several counties not placed under official Blizzard Warnings until rather late in the game. For sure, one of the biggest takeaways/reminders from this event was that even minimal snowfall can wreak havoc on travel in the presence of powerful winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on surrounding observations, snowfall totals ranged from 1 to 2 inches across the county, and combined with wind gusts near 60 MPH, blizzard conditions occurred. A total of only 0.8 inch was reported at the Grand Island Central Nebraska Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between the later evening of Monday the 14th and the early evening of Tuesday the 15th, most of South Central Nebraska received at least a shallow coating of snow, although only a handful of counties in the western half of the area reported amounts as high as 3-5 inches. Within these five counties (Dawson, Buffalo, Gosper, Furnas, Phelps), a few of the highest CoCoRaHS/NWS cooperative observer measurements included: 5 at Sumner and six miles southwest of Lexington, 4.5 six miles south-southeast of Lexington, 4.1 four miles south of Cozad, 3.8 at Cambridge and 3.2 eight miles south of Elwood. Outside of these five counties, the vast majority of the remainder of South Central Nebraska received anywhere from a light dusting up to around 2. Winds were not much of a factor, with sustained speeds only averaging 5-15 MPH. Temperatures were also not overly-cold through this event, mainly low-mid 20s F. ||Breaking down event evolution and timing, snow got underway in western local counties mainly between 9 p.m. and midnight, and then continued intermittently throughout much of the morning and afternoon of the 15th while gradually expanding eastward to affect more of the area. Within the western counties that received the most snow, the majority of accumulation occurred by noon, but at least occasional light snow/flurries hung around until late in the afternoon. In the mid-upper levels, the main player behind this relatively low-key snow event was a shortwave trough traversing eastward across the central United States, but with the primary closed low/vorticity maximum focused a bit south of the local area, generally along the Kansas-Oklahoma border. At the surface, the relatively light winds across South Central Nebraska were largely a result of the primary surface low pressure system remaining hundreds of miles to the south-southwest (mainly over eastern New Mexico/west Texas).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals from area CoCoRaHS observers included 5 inches from 6 miles southwest of Lexington as well as from Sumner, 4.1 inches from 4 miles south of Cozad, and 3.5 inches from 1 mile east of Lexington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For roughly 24 hours between the very early mornings of Friday the 11th and Saturday the 12th, much of South Central Nebraska saw its first 3-inch-or-greater snowfall of the 2020-21 season, including the Tri Cities. The highest amounts mainly in the 3-6 range (but localized higher) concentrated within a southwest-to-northeast swath bisecting the heart of the area, generally 20 miles either side of a line from near Cambridge-Minden-Doniphan-Shelby. Within this zone, a sampling of the highest CoCoRaHS/NWS cooperative observer measurements featured: 8.1 three miles northeast of Shelby, 6.5 at Stromsburg, 6.4 in Grand Island, 6.3 in Holdrege and 6.0 at Osceola. Again though, most places within the main corridor checked in between 3-5, including 5.1 at NWS Hastings, 4.8 at Cambridge, 4.4 three miles north of York and 3.5 at Kearney airport. Meanwhile, both north and south of the primary snow band, totals dropped off considerably to no more than 1-2 (including places such as Ord, Greeley, Superior and Hebron). North winds were not overly-impactful, but strong enough to cause some minor blowing/drifting thanks to sustained speeds typically 15-20 MPH and occasional gusts around 30 MPH. While snow was by far the dominant precipitation type, a few observers (including at Osceola) reported a brief period of sleet/graupel early on. Temperatures during this event hovered within a few degrees either side of freezing. ||Timing-wise, while at least light snow/flurries affected much of the area for roughly 24 hours, the vast majority of steadier snow and accumulation occurred during the daytime and evening hours of the 11th. Forcing in the mid-upper levels was clearly tied to a modestly-strong shortwave trough tracking east through the Central Plains, probably most evident at 700 millibars as a closed low slid along/just south of the Nebraska-Kansas border. At the surface, the steady (but not overly-strong) north winds were driven by a modest pressure gradient between a broad high centered over the Dakotas/south-central Canada, and a fairly weak low that gradually drifted northeast from Oklahoma toward central Illinois (central pressure around 1006 millibars early evening on the 11th).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 4.4 inches was measured by a Cooperative Observer located 3 miles north of York. A CoCoRaHS observer located 6 miles north of Bradshaw measured 5.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between the later evening of Monday the 14th and the early evening of Tuesday the 15th, most of South Central Nebraska received at least a shallow coating of snow, although only a handful of counties in the western half of the area reported amounts as high as 3-5 inches. Within these five counties (Dawson, Buffalo, Gosper, Furnas, Phelps), a few of the highest CoCoRaHS/NWS cooperative observer measurements included: 5 at Sumner and six miles southwest of Lexington, 4.5 six miles south-southeast of Lexington, 4.1 four miles south of Cozad, 3.8 at Cambridge and 3.2 eight miles south of Elwood. Outside of these five counties, the vast majority of the remainder of South Central Nebraska received anywhere from a light dusting up to around 2. Winds were not much of a factor, with sustained speeds only averaging 5-15 MPH. Temperatures were also not overly-cold through this event, mainly low-mid 20s F. ||Breaking down event evolution and timing, snow got underway in western local counties mainly between 9 p.m. and midnight, and then continued intermittently throughout much of the morning and afternoon of the 15th while gradually expanding eastward to affect more of the area. Within the western counties that received the most snow, the majority of accumulation occurred by noon, but at least occasional light snow/flurries hung around until late in the afternoon. In the mid-upper levels, the main player behind this relatively low-key snow event was a shortwave trough traversing eastward across the central United States, but with the primary closed low/vorticity maximum focused a bit south of the local area, generally along the Kansas-Oklahoma border. At the surface, the relatively light winds across South Central Nebraska were largely a result of the primary surface low pressure system remaining hundreds of miles to the south-southwest (mainly over eastern New Mexico/west Texas).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals were estimated to range from 2 to 4 inches across the northern half of the county (based on nearby reports and NOHRSC Snowfall Analysis).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The far southwestern portion of Nebraska saw non-thunderstorm high sustained winds and gusts associated with a cold front beginning during the overnight hours of December 22nd that continued until the  afternoon hours of December 23rd. The highest sustained winds were recorded 2 miles northwest of Haigler, NE at 45 mph while the highest wind gust was recorded at the McCook, NE airport of 70 mph. These winds caused reduced visibilities in the area due to blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to a cold front, high winds up to 40 mph sustained and wind gusts up to 70 mph were observed in Red Willow County from the night of 12/22/20 through the morning of 12/23/20. Both the max sustained winds of 40 mph and the max wind gust of 70 mph were recorded at the McCook, NE ASOS. Blowing snow caused by the high winds reduced visibility down to 1/2 mile in some areas on the morning of 12/23/20. Key impacts: blowing snow, high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During December, 20 of the 24 counties in South Central Nebraska were either completely or partially-included in Severe Drought (D2)-or-worse, per weekly issuances of the U.S. Drought Monitor. Of these 20 counties, 18 of them spent the entire month in either Severe (D2) or Extreme Drought (D3), while portions of western Merrick and far western Nance counties were added into the D2 mix starting on the 8th. Overall though, and not surprisingly given this was a winter month (and non-growing season), drought categories changed very little during December. However, the mainly status quo (and not worsening) drought situation could also be credited to some positive precipitation trends, as roughly two-thirds of the area received at least slightly above normal monthly totals (commonly 0.65-1.10) and roughly 80 percent of the area reported at least one-half inch (a respectable amount by December standards). In fact, for much of the western half of the area (generally west of Highway 281), December ended a four-month stretch of solidly below normal precipitation that had defined August-November.||Detailing specific drought categories during December (and going from worst to best): There was no change whatsoever in the areal coverage of D3, which continued to include all of Furnas/Gosper counties, along with adjacent portions of Dawson/Phelps/Harlan/far southwestern Buffalo (total of six affected counties). To the east-northeast of there, all-or-some of 14 counties (but especially those along/west of Highway 281) spent all-or-most of the month in D2. Meanwhile, the remaining four far-eastern local counties (along the Highway 81 corridor) largely maintained Moderate Drought (D1) status during December, although much of Thayer and far southeastern Fillmore/Nuckolls counties actually improved to Abnormally Dry (D0) early in the month (effectively reverting back to what was assigned there in late-October). Per several dozen NWS cooperative observers/official stations, a few of the overall-highest December precipitation totals within South Central Nebraska featured: 1.51 at Osceola, 1.30 three miles northeast of Shelby and 1.20 at Grand Island (Central Nebraska Regional Airport). In contrast, a few of the lowest (and below normal) tallies included: 0.26 at Harlan County Lake, 0.29 in Red Cloud and 0.29 in Franklin. Temperature-wise, although certainly not as big of a drought-factor as it would have been during the growing season, December was the second-straight warmer-than-average month, with most local stations averaging around 5 degrees F above normal.

During December, 20 of the 24 counties in South Central Nebraska were either completely or partially-included in Severe Drought (D2)-or-worse, per weekly issuances of the U.S. Drought Monitor. Of these 20 counties, 18 of them spent the entire month in either Severe (D2) or Extreme Drought (D3), while portions of western Merrick and far western Nance counties were added into the D2 mix starting on the 8th. Overall though, and not surprisingly given this was a winter month (and non-growing season), drought categories changed very little during December. However, the mainly status quo (and not worsening) drought situation could also be credited to some positive precipitation trends, as roughly two-thirds of the area received at least slightly above normal monthly totals (commonly 0.65-1.10) and roughly 80 percent of the area reported at least one-half inch (a respectable amount by December standards). In fact, for much of the western half of the area (generally west of Highway 281), December ended a four-month stretch of solidly below normal precipitation that had defined August-November.||Detailing specific drought categories during December (and going from worst to best): There was no change whatsoever in the areal coverage of D3, which continued to include all of Furnas/Gosper counties, along with adjacent portions of Dawson/Phelps/Harlan/far southwestern Buffalo (total of six affected counties). To the east-northeast of there, all-or-some of 14 counties (but especially those along/west of Highway 281) spent all-or-most of the month in D2. Meanwhile, the remaining four far-eastern local counties (along the Highway 81 corridor) largely maintained Moderate Drought (D1) status during December, although much of Thayer and far southeastern Fillmore/Nuckolls counties actually improved to Abnormally Dry (D0) early in the month (effectively reverting back to what was assigned there in late-October). Per several dozen NWS cooperative observers/official stations, a few of the overall-highest December precipitation totals within South Central Nebraska featured: 1.51 at Osceola, 1.30 three miles northeast of Shelby and 1.20 at Grand Island (Central Nebraska Regional Airport). In contrast, a few of the lowest (and below normal) tallies included: 0.26 at Harlan County Lake, 0.29 in Red Cloud and 0.29 in Franklin. Temperature-wise, although certainly not as big of a drought-factor as it would have been during the growing season, December was the second-straight warmer-than-average month, with most local stations averaging around 5 degrees F above normal.

Only a few days after South Central Nebraska had melted off the vast majority of lingering snow cover from earlier in the month, a moderately-strong winter storm rolled through between the evening of Monday the 28th and afternoon of Tuesday the 29th, dropping a fresh blanket of 2-8 inches of snow in most locales, followed by a light coating of sleet and freezing rain/drizzle especially in southeast counties. Overall though, snow was clearly the main story, especially within northern counties along/north of Highway 92 where the highest amounts of 5-8 concentrated, including CoCoRaHS/NWS cooperative observer totals of: 7.4 in St. Paul and Ord, 6.8 at Greeley, 6.5 in Loup City and also two miles west of Genoa, and 6.1 in North Loup. Southward from there, slightly-lower amounts of 3-5 prevailed within counties along the Interstate 80/Highway 6 corridors, including totals of 5 at Sumner, 4.1 at NWS Hastings, 4.0 in Grand Island and 3.5 in Clay Center and Geneva. Farther south yet, the overall-lowest snowfall of 1-3 fell within far southern counties along the Kansas border. However, especially areas in/near Nuckolls and Thayer counties saw some steadier periods of light freezing rain and sleet showers during the latter stages of the event, although ice accumulation was generally only around one-tenth of an inch and potential travel issues solely from icing were likely muted given that it fell onto freshly-fallen snow. Locations farther north and west (including the Tri Cities area) also saw a light wintry mix of sleet and freezing drizzle in the wake of departing snow, but icing amounted to just a light glaze and the travel impacts from snow-covered roads were far more of a factor (though no closures of main highways occurred). Winds were not overly-strong during the main period of snow, but steady east-southeast speeds of 10-20 MPH with occasional gusts as high as 25-30 MPH were enough to cause some modest blowing/drifting. Temperatures during most of the event hovered in the 20s F.||Taking a closer look at how things unfolded, the majority of snow accumulation occurred between 9 p.m. on the 28th and sunrise on the 29th, as a northwest-to-southeast oriented band of moderate to briefly-heavy snow steadily advanced across South Central Nebraska from southwest-to-northeast. This primary band was driven by a fairly classic case of low-mid level isentropic lift and mid level frontogenesis (especially evident at 700 millibars), with this forcing focused downstream from a larger-scale, positively-tilted trough positioned from the Desert Southwest into the Central Rockies. However, things changed character near and especially after sunrise on the 29th in the wake of the main snow band, as the combination of lower-level warm air advection and an intrusion of drier mid-level air from the southwest (with resultant steepening lapse rates) allowed precipitation to transition from predominantly snow to mainly isolated to scattered sleet showers and areas of light freezing rain/drizzle. Any steadier/measurable precipitation gradually departed the majority of the local area from west-to-east between sunrise and noon, although pesky light freezing drizzle hung around in some places until early evening. At the surface, the modestly-breezy east-southeast winds that peaked between midnight-noon were driven by a decent pressure gradient between a strong/expansive 1035 millibar high departing eastbound through the Midwest and a 1008 millibar low centered over Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow amounts ranged from 5 to 8 inches across the county. Cooperative Observer reports included 7.4 inches at Ord, 6.1 inches at North Loup, and 5.1 inches from 2 miles west of Arcadia. CoCoRaHS observer reports included 6.7 inches from 5 miles southwest of Elyria and 5.0 inches from 9 miles northwest of Ord.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thanks to a somewhat-unexpectedly potent combination of intense northwest winds and relatively-minimal snow, mainly the daytime hours on this Wednesday featured several hours of near-blizzard to outright-blizzard conditions across most of South Central Nebraska, shutting down several highways for a time and snarling pre-Christmas travel. Various law enforcement agencies reported dozens of traffic accidents and slide-offs (fortunately none fatal within the local area) and the following main highways were reported closed at various times: Interstate 80 between York and Kearney, Highway 281 between Grand Island and Hastings, Highway 30 from Grand Island-Kearney, Highway 34 from Grand Island-York, Highway 6 from Hastings-Kenesaw and Highway 81 near Shelby (all had re-opened by late afternoon). In Adams County, snowplows were pulled off the road for several hours due to very poor visibility. As for areal coverage of white-out conditions, the worst/longest-lasting issues focused within counties both along/north of Highway 6 and also along/east of Highway 183 (including the Tri Cities). Within this area, most places endured at least semi-frequent blizzard conditions for 6-8 hours while accumulating 1-3 of snow, while far northern counties (such as Valley/Greeley) saw blizzard conditions for as long as 10-12 hours and received slightly-higher snow amounts up to 3-4. Meanwhile, the situation was not as severe within far southern/southwest counties especially near the Kansas border, where snow was lighter/less-intense compared to farther north (amounts mainly under 1) and didn't last as long. Although the magnitude of wintry precipitation/blizzard impacts varied somewhat across South Central Nebraska, all 24 counties dealt with at least 9-12 hours of feisty northwest winds, commonly sustained 30-40 MPH and gusting 45-60+ MPH. A few of the highest official peak gusts at airports included: 76 MPH at Ord and 61 MPH at Grand Island, Hastings and York. While there were no known reports of substantial wind damage, NWS observers near both Arcadia and Shelby noted that several small tree branches were downed. ||Getting into more detail regarding event timing and meteorological background, the main driving force in the mid-upper levels was the passage of a deep, slightly-positively-tilted trough, with its primary vorticity maximum tracking from the Central Rockies on the evening of the 22nd, and then across the heart of Nebraska-Kansas during the day on the 23rd before departing into the Upper Midwest/Upper Great Lakes through the evening-overnight. At the surface, the associated low pressure center gradually intensified from around 994 millibars as it crossed Nebraska during the overnight-early morning of the 22nd-23rd, to 988 millibars by mid-day in the IA-MN-WI border area, and then maintained intensity while reaching Lake Superior overnight. Locally, the initial surge of strong northwest winds crossed South Central Nebraska during the pre-dawn of the 23rd behind the main synoptic cold front, but gusts largely held under 50 MPH. The overall-strongest winds ended up occurring between mid-morning and mid-afternoon, corresponding to the peak surface pressure gradient between the departing/deepening low and strong high pressure nosing in from the west-northwest (for a few hours the west-to-east gradient across the state of Nebraska averaged near 25 millibars!) As for snowfall timing, an initial, narrow band swept across most of the area during the pre-dawn hours, but the primary, broad zone of mainly light-to-moderate snow (with brief heavier bursts) gradually overspread South Central Nebraska from west-northwest to east-southeast during the morning, eventually departing in similar fashion during the afternoon. Actual falling/accumulating snow ended across most of the area between 2-4 p.m., but lingering blowing snow and ground blizzard conditions held on for a few more hours...until gusts diminished more consistently to around 30 MPH-or-less after nightfall. From a forecast review perspective, the intense winds and at least minor blowing snow impacts were fairly well-anticipated, as a High Wind Warning was issued for the entire area 12-18 hours in advance and a Winter Weather Advisory (for the majority of the area) was issued 6-18 hours out. However, especially the southward extent of accumulating snow and the resultant potential for all-out blizzard conditions was not as well foreseen (especially as far south as the Interstate 80/Highway 6 corridors), with several counties not placed under official Blizzard Warnings until rather late in the game. For sure, one of the biggest takeaways/reminders from this event was that even minimal snowfall can wreak havoc on travel in the presence of powerful winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periodic gusts exceeding 60 MPH and sustained speeds near 40 MPH occurred across the county. The peak gust of the day was 76 MPH, which was recorded at 11:51 AM by the Ord Airport ASOS. The NWS cooperative observer near Arcadia reported several small tree limbs blown down. Also, the intense winds, combined with snow, promoted blizzard conditions.

At the surface, the associated low pressure center gradually intensified from around 994 millibars as it crossed Nebraska during the overnight-early morning of the 22nd-23rd, to 988 millibars by mid-day in the IA-MN-WI border area, and then maintained intensity while reaching Lake Superior overnight. Locally, the initial surge of strong northwest winds crossed South Central Nebraska during the pre-dawn of the 23rd behind the main synoptic cold front, but gusts largely held under 50 MPH. The overall-strongest winds ended up occurring between mid-morning and mid-afternoon, corresponding to the peak surface pressure gradient between the departing/deepening low and strong high pressure nosing in from the west-northwest (for a few hours the west-to-east gradient across the state of Nebraska averaged near 25 millibars!) As for snowfall timing, an initial, narrow band swept across most of the area during the pre-dawn hours, but the primary, broad zone of mainly light-to-moderate snow (with brief heavier bursts) gradually overspread South Central Nebraska from west-northwest to east-southeast during the morning, eventually departing in similar fashion during the afternoon. Actual falling/accumulating snow ended across most of the area between 2-4 p.m., but lingering blowing snow and ground blizzard conditions held on for a few more hours...until gusts diminished more consistently to around 30 MPH-or-less after nightfall. From a forecast review perspective, the intense winds and at least minor blowing snow impacts were fairly well-anticipated, as a High Wind Warning was issued for the entire area 12-18 hours in advance and a Winter Weather Advisory (for the majority of the area) was issued 6-18 hours out. However, especially the southward extent of accumulating snow and the resultant potential for all-out blizzard conditions was not as well foreseen (especially as far south as the Interstate 80/Highway 6 corridors), with several counties not placed under official Blizzard Warnings until rather late in the game. For sure, one of the biggest takeaways/reminders from this event was that even minimal snowfall can wreak havoc on travel in the presence of powerful winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed in eastern Colorado during the afternoon moving northeast behind an outflow boundary. As the storms entered into southwest Nebraska, they merged with thunderstorms that developed in northeast Colorado to form a line of storms along the outflow boundary. This line of storms produced hail up to penny sized sized in Dundy County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to penny sized hail reported at the location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms extended south into Southwest Nebraska behind a weak cold front moving eastward.  The largest hail reported was baseball size near Palisade.  The same storms broke limbs off of trees at the same location.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Also came with estimated gusts of 70 MPH that blew down tree limbs.
